strategies.name: SEP-IRA / Solo 401(k) for self-employed filer
strategies.trigger: netSeIncome >= 30000 AND no existing SEP/Solo401k retirement adjustment AND filingStatus != married_filing_separately
strategies.formula: contribution = min((netSeIncome - halfSeDeduction) * 0.20, planLimit); estSavings = contribution * (federalMarginalRate + stateMarginalRate)
prerequisiteData: Whether the client has any other retirement plan (SIMPLE, 401(k))
Whether the client wants employee-deferral capacity (Solo 401(k)) vs employer-only (SEP)
strategies.action: Open and fund a SEP-IRA (or Solo 401(k) if employee-deferral desired) by the extended return deadline. Contribution = ~{{contribution}}; estimated combined-rate savings ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Pass-Through Entity Tax (PTET) election to bypass SALT cap
strategies.irsPub: IRS Notice 2020-75; state-specific PTET statute. OBBBA (P.L. 119-21 §70120) added IRC §164(b)(7): the SALT cap is $40,000 ($20,000 MFS) for TY2025 [$40,400 TY2026, +1%/yr through 2029, reverting to $10,000 after 2029], phasing DOWN 30% of MAGI over $500,000 ($250,000 MFS) to a $10,000 floor.
strategies.trigger: K-1 pass-through income > 0 AND resident state has PTET regime AND uncapped SALT exceeds the year-indexed federal SALT cap ($10k TCJA TY2024; $40k OBBBA TY2025, phasing down above $500k MAGI)
strategies.formula: estSavings = max(0, actualSALT - federalSaltCap) * federalMarginalRate, where federalSaltCap is year-indexed + MAGI-phase-down-adjusted (TCJA $10k for TY2024; OBBBA $40k/$40.4k for TY2025/2026 reduced 30% of MAGI over $500k, $10k floor).
prerequisiteData: Confirm entity is eligible for PTET in its state (typically S-corp or partnership)
Confirm all owners consent to PTET election where state requires unanimous consent
Estimated state tax payable from the entity
MAGI (drives the OBBBA SALT-cap phase-down above $500k / $250k MFS)
strategies.action: Have the pass-through entity make a PTET election for the next tax year so state income tax is paid + deducted at the entity level, bypassing the federal SALT cap (OBBBA §164(b)(7): $40k for TY2025, phasing down to $10k for high earners). Estimated federal benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Bunch itemized deductions in alternate years
strategies.trigger: itemizedTotal between 85% and 115% of stdDed AND charitableCash > 0
strategies.formula: estSavings = stdDed * 0.5 * marginalRate * 0.5 (half-effect over a 2-year alternating cycle)
prerequisiteData: Whether client can prepay next-year charitable gifts in December (cash basis)
Whether client has flexibility on property-tax timing
strategies.action: Stack two years of charitable cash giving into one tax year (e.g. December prepay + January next year). Take the standard deduction in the off-year. Estimated avg annual benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Roth conversion window — fill the bracket
strategies.trigger: federalMarginalRate < 0.24 AND taxpayerAge BETWEEN 30 AND 72 (or unknown)
strategies.formula: conversion = next-bracket headroom; estSavings = conversion * (expectedFutureRate - currentRate). expectedFutureRate placeholder 0.32.
prerequisiteData: Traditional IRA balance
Expected future marginal rate (CPA judgment call)
Whether client expects to itemize in conversion year
strategies.action: Convert ~{{conversion}} of traditional IRA to Roth this year — taxable at the current marginal rate (~{{currentRate}}) but tax-free forever in Roth. Net estimated benefit ~{{estSavings}} assuming a future rate of 32%.

strategies.name: AMT timing — spread ISO exercises or same-year disqualifying sale
strategies.trigger: amtTax > 0 AND amt_iso_bargain_element adjustment > 0
strategies.formula: estSavings = amtTax (entirely deferrable by spreading exercises across years OR disqualifying sale in same year)
prerequisiteData: ISO exercise schedule and grant dates
Whether client is willing to do a same-year disqualifying sale (loses LTCG character)
strategies.action: Restructure the ISO exercise plan: spread exercises across tax years to stay below the AMT exemption phase-in, OR execute a same-year disqualifying sale to convert bargain element from AMT preference to ordinary W-2 income. Estimated AMT eliminated ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: NIIT cliff avoidance — get AGI below the 3.8% threshold
strategies.trigger: AGI within +/- $10,000 of NIIT threshold AND netInvestmentIncome > 0
strategies.formula: estSavings = niitTax (currently paid; defer ordinary income or accelerate deductions to drop AGI below threshold)
prerequisiteData: Deferrable income (year-end bonus, RSU vest timing)
Acceleratable deductions (charitable cash, HSA, 401(k) employee deferral)
strategies.action: Drop AGI below the NIIT threshold of {{threshold}} by deferring ~{{deferAmount}} of ordinary income to next year OR accelerating an HSA/401(k)/charitable contribution. Estimated NIIT saved ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: §199A QBI deduction — wage/UBIA limit planning
strategies.irsPub: Pub 535; Form 8995-A. Taxable-income thresholds: $191,950/$383,900 TY2024 (Rev. Proc. 2023-34); $197,300/$394,600 TY2025; $201,750/$403,500 TY2026 (Rev. Proc. 2025-32). OBBBA (P.L. 119-21) made §199A PERMANENT (no sunset), WIDENED the phase-in range to $75k single / $150k MFJ (TY2026, from $50k/$100k), and added a NEW $400 minimum QBI deduction for taxpayers with >= $1,000 of active QBI (TY2026+, indexed thereafter).
strategies.trigger: K-1 pass-through income > 0 AND taxable income in §199A phase-in window (year-indexed: $50k single / $100k MFJ band through TY2025; $75k / $150k TY2026 under OBBBA)
strategies.formula: estSavings = qbi * 0.20 * federalMarginalRate * fraction_lost_in_phase_in (proxy 0.50 within band)
prerequisiteData: W-2 wages paid by the pass-through entity (Box 1)
UBIA of qualified property held by the entity
Whether activity is a Specified Service Trade or Business (SSTB)
strategies.action: Within the §199A phase-in band, restructure W-2 wages and/or property holdings inside the entity to qualify for the wage/UBIA-based limit on QBI. Estimated recovered QBI deduction value ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Charitable Donor-Advised Fund (DAF) bunching
strategies.trigger: charitableCash > 5000 AND federalMarginalRate >= 0.32
strategies.formula: estSavings = (charitableCash * 2) * marginalRate * 0.20 (3-year bunching shifts std-ded cliff by ~20% of stacked total)
prerequisiteData: Whether the client already has a DAF account
Cash-flow capacity for a 2-3 year forward gift
strategies.action: Open a DAF and front-load 2-3 years of charitable cash into one tax year to clear the standard-deduction cliff, then distribute the grants over time. Estimated benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Tax-loss harvesting against $3k ordinary offset
strategies.trigger: capitalLossDeducted < 3000 AND (netLTCG > 0 OR 1099-B activity present)
strategies.formula: estSavings = (3000 - capitalLossDeducted) * ordinaryMarginalRate (the $3k ordinary offset; carryforward beyond)
prerequisiteData: Unrealized loss inventory in brokerage account(s)
Whether client trades in a wash-sale-prone way (would defeat the harvest)
strategies.action: Before year-end, realize ~$3,000 of net long-term or short-term capital losses from underperforming positions; reinvest in a non-substantially-identical security. Estimated savings ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Foreign Tax Credit claim vs. deduction
strategies.trigger: foreign_tax_paid > 0 AND foreignTaxCredit.credit < foreign_tax_paid * 0.95
strategies.formula: estSavings = foreign_tax_paid - foreignTaxCredit.credit (direct $-for-$ credit vs Sch A deduction)
prerequisiteData: Country-by-country foreign tax paid (for Form 1116 Schedule B if > $20k)
Foreign-source taxable income for the actual Form 1116 limit
strategies.action: Claim foreign taxes paid as a Foreign Tax Credit on Form 1116 instead of (or in addition to) an itemized deduction. Estimated additional savings ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Qualified Charitable Distribution (QCD) for age 70½+
strategies.trigger: taxpayerAge >= 70.5 AND 1099-R retirementIncome > 0 AND charitable_cash adjustment > 0
strategies.formula: Direct trustee-to-charity transfer (max $105k TY2024 / $108k TY2025 / $111k TY2026); excluded from AGI. Savings = AGI reduction × marginal rate + NIIT/IRMAA/SS-taxability spill-overs that the engine captures via H2.
prerequisiteData: Confirm IRA owner is age 70½+ on the distribution date (Form 8606 / DOB)
Confirm charity is a 501(c)(3) public charity (NOT a private foundation or DAF)
Transfer must go DIRECT from IRA custodian to charity — not through the client
strategies.action: For up to {{qcdAmount}} of TY{{taxYear}} charitable giving, direct the IRA custodian to make a Qualified Charitable Distribution. Reduces AGI by ~{{qcdAmount}}; engine-verified federal+state savings ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Donate appreciated stock instead of cash
strategies.trigger: charitable_cash > $5,000 AND long-term capital gains > 0
strategies.formula: Donating appreciated stock (held >1 year) at FMV: same deduction, BUT avoids cap-gains tax on the unrealized appreciation. estSavings = min(charitable_cash, LTCG) × LTCG_rate × assumed_unrealized_gain_pct.
prerequisiteData: Lots of appreciated stock with >1 year holding period
Cost basis of donated lots (for Form 8283)
Confirm charity accepts in-kind stock transfers
strategies.action: Instead of donating {{donationAmount}} in cash, transfer the same FMV of long-held appreciated stock. Get the same charitable deduction AND avoid ~{{estSavings}} of capital-gains tax on the unrealized appreciation.

strategies.name: Augusta Rule — rent home to your business §280A(g)
strategies.trigger: Self-employment / S-corp / partnership income > $50,000 AND no existing augusta_rule_rent adjustment
strategies.formula: Rent personal residence to business for ≤14 days/yr at fair rental rate. Business deducts; homeowner excludes from income per §280A(g) safe harbor. estSavings = (14 × dailyRate) × federal+state marginal rate. Default dailyRate $1,500.
prerequisiteData: Document fair market rental rate for area (Airbnb / venue comps)
Calendar legitimate business purpose for each rental day (board meetings, client events)
Strict 14-day annual limit — track via written rental agreement
strategies.action: Rent your residence to your business for up to 14 days at ~{{dailyRate}}/day = ~{{rentAmount}} total. Business deducts; you exclude under §280A(g). Estimated savings ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: NUA — Net Unrealized Appreciation on employer stock
strategies.trigger: H5 asset with assetType=employer_stock_in_401k AND nuaEligible=true AND balance > $50,000
strategies.formula: Lump-sum distribution of employer stock in-kind. Ordinary tax on COST BASIS only; unrealized appreciation (NUA) gets LTCG treatment when sold. estSavings = (FMV - costBasis) × (marginal - LTCG rate).
prerequisiteData: Plan document confirming NUA-eligible lump-sum distribution permitted
Cost basis of employer stock in 401(k) — usually from plan statement
Client's marginal tax rate at distribution (must do entire-plan distribution in one tax year)
Triggering event: separation from service / age 59½ / death / disability
strategies.action: Execute a lump-sum distribution of employer stock from the 401(k) in-kind. Pay ordinary income tax on ~{{costBasis}} (cost basis). The unrealized appreciation of ~{{nuaAmount}} gets LTCG treatment when shares are sold — saving ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Mega-Backdoor Roth via after-tax 401(k)
strategies.trigger: H5 asset with assetType=401k_after_tax (presence indicates plan permits after-tax + in-service rollover/conversion) AND headroom = §415(c) limit - (pre-tax + employer match) > $0
strategies.formula: After-tax 401(k) contributions converted in-service to Roth 401(k) or rolled to Roth IRA via Notice 2014-54. estSavings = contribution × future tax-free growth × assumed future rate.
prerequisiteData: Plan document confirming after-tax contributions + in-service rollover OR in-plan Roth conversion permitted
Current year pre-tax + employer match contributions (subtract from §415(c) cap to find headroom)
Client age (catch-up only applies for §402(g) elective deferrals, NOT after-tax)
Client's expected future tax rate at distribution (drives long-term value)
strategies.action: Contribute up to {{contribution}} of after-tax dollars to the 401(k), then immediately convert/roll to Roth. Tax-free growth + tax-free distributions. Long-term estimated benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: S-corp reasonable compensation split
strategies.trigger: Active S-corp K-1 income > $50,000 (entityType=s_corp on K-1, activityType=active) AND no W-2 from same EIN
strategies.formula: Split S-corp net income between W-2 wages (FICA-subject) and distributions (FICA-exempt). estSavings = (distributions × 15.3% FICA up to SS wage base + 2.9% Medicare uncapped) — recovered after CPA-validated reasonable comp benchmark.
prerequisiteData: Reasonable comp benchmark for client's role (RC Reports / BLS / industry surveys)
Time spent on business (full-time vs part-time)
Geographic location (cost-of-labor adjustment)
Confirm S-corp election is active (Form 2553 + IRS acceptance)
strategies.action: Adopt a reasonable-comp split: pay yourself ~{{reasonableComp}} as W-2 wages; take ~{{distributions}} as distributions. Estimated FICA savings ~{{estSavings}} per year.

strategies.name: Real Estate Professional Status (REPS) election
strategies.trigger: Has rental real estate income/expenses AND combined SE/W-2 < $200k AND no current REPS election AND has passive losses being suspended
strategies.formula: REPS converts rental from passive to non-passive — losses offset W-2/SE income (no $25k PAL cap). estSavings = currently-suspended PAL × federal+state marginal rate.
prerequisiteData: Documented 750+ hours/year in real-property trade or business
More than 50% of total work time in real estate
Per-property election to aggregate (Reg §1.469-9(g)) for material participation
Detailed time logs (calendar, contemporaneous records — IRS scrutinizes)
strategies.action: Elect Real Estate Professional Status under §469(c)(7). Aggregate rentals under §1.469-9(g) for material participation. Currently-suspended passive losses (~{{suspendedPal}}) convert to active deductions. Estimated savings ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Charitable Remainder Trust (CRT) — irrevocable split-interest gift
strategies.trigger: Highly-appreciated asset (low basis vs high FMV) + charitable intent + age 55+ (income stream) — heuristic informational only.
strategies.formula: Transfer asset to CRT → no capital gains on sale by CRT → annual annuity (CRAT) or unitrust (CRUT) income stream → charitable income tax deduction (PV of remainder) → remainder to charity at term end. estSavings = (avoided cap gains tax) + (deduction × marginal rate) — discounted.
prerequisiteData: Asset to donate (real estate / appreciated stock / closely-held business interest)
Cost basis and FMV — must have substantial appreciation to justify
Annuity/unitrust election (CRAT fixed vs CRUT % of FMV)
Beneficiary's age (drives §7520 PV calculation)
Charity selection (501(c)(3) public or DAF)
Trust attorney + actuarial valuation (complex — 6-12 month setup)
strategies.action: Engage a trust attorney to draft a CRT. Transfer the appreciated asset; CRT sells tax-free; client receives annuity/unitrust income stream + immediate charitable deduction for the present value of the remainder interest. Heuristic savings estimate available only after the §7520 actuarial calculation.

strategies.name: §263A Inventory Method Choice — small-biz cash method
strategies.trigger: Net SE > $100,000 (inventory-business proxy) AND no existing inventory_method signal. Heuristic — engine cannot verify whether business actually carries inventory.
strategies.formula: Small business exception §263A(i): gross receipts under the §448(c) threshold (3-yr avg) can elect cash method + skip UNICAP indirect cost capitalization. §448(c) threshold is inflation-indexed: $30M TY2024 / $31M TY2025 / $32M TY2026 (Rev. Proc. 2025-32). Accelerates COGS deduction (no opening inventory addback) and saves admin cost. estSavings = ~$10,000 typical acceleration × marginal rate.
prerequisiteData: Confirm 3-yr average gross receipts under the §448(c) threshold ($30M TY2024 / $31M TY2025 / $32M TY2026 — indexed per Rev. Proc.; was $25M pre-2018)
Confirm business actually carries inventory (vs pure service)
Current accounting method: cash vs accrual (Form 3115 required to change)
Form 3115 (Change in Accounting Method) — §481(a) catch-up adjustment spread over 4 yrs (or 1 yr if favorable)
Note: small-biz cash-method election bypasses §263A UNICAP indirect cost capitalization — significant admin simplification
Method choice is generally LOCKED IN — opt out requires Form 3115 again with valid business purpose
strategies.action: If business has gross receipts under the §448(c) threshold (3-yr avg; $30M TY2024 / $31M TY2025 / $32M TY2026), elect cash method via Form 3115 + skip §263A UNICAP. Saves ~{{estSavings}} from acceleration + administrative simplification.

strategies.name: Lot Rotation — tax-efficient retirement withdrawal sequence
strategies.trigger: Age >= 60 AND has H5 mix of (traditional_ira OR 401k_traditional) AND (roth_ira OR 401k_roth) AND (brokerage_taxable) — retiree with diversified account types.
strategies.formula: Optimal withdrawal sequence for retirees: (1) taxable brokerage first (LTCG-favored at sale + step-up at death) → (2) tax-deferred trad IRA / 401(k) (ordinary tax at marginal) → (3) Roth IRA / Roth 401(k) LAST (tax-free + no RMD § estate-tax-free to heirs). Sequence preserves tax-advantaged growth longer. estSavings = $20,000/yr × 10 yr × 2% rate optimization = $4,000/yr.
prerequisiteData: Confirm projected total retirement annual spend
Compute RMD age (73 SECURE 2.0; pushed to 75 in TY2033)
Each account balance + projected growth rate + tax character (ordinary vs LTCG vs tax-free)
Tax bracket projection by withdrawal year
Health-care needs (HSA before age 65 — qualified medical tax-free)
Charitable intent — QCD (G1.11) integrates with this sequence post-70.5
Estate planning — Roth IRA preserved for heirs as stretch-eligible (10-yr rule post-SECURE 1.0 for non-spouse)
Roth conversion timing — pre-RMD-age conversion ladder (G1.22) coordinates with this sequence
strategies.action: Coordinate retirement withdrawals: deplete taxable brokerage FIRST (LTCG-favored), then tax-deferred (trad IRA/401k), reserve Roth for LAST (tax-free + no RMD + estate-favored). Annual benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: §168(k) Bonus Depreciation Election OUT (low-income year) — POST-OBBBA
strategies.trigger: Has bonus_depreciation_basis adjustment > 0 AND taxable income < $50,000 (low-income year proxy). Election-out preserves the deduction for higher-bracket future years.
strategies.formula: §168(k)(7) annual election OUT of bonus depreciation. Property recovers under regular MACRS instead (5/7/27.5/39 yr). Useful when current marginal rate is LOW (12%) and projected future rate HIGHER (22-24%). estSavings = $20,000 bonus dep × (24% future − 12% current) = $2,400 bracket arbitrage. POST-OBBBA: 100% bonus is now PERMANENT for property placed in service AFTER 2025-01-19 (reverses TCJA phasedown). Strategy still relevant for low-income year bracket arbitrage; election-out always available.
prerequisiteData: Verify client has §168(k) bonus-eligible property placed in service this year
POST-OBBBA bonus rate (property placed in service AFTER 2025-01-19): 100% PERMANENT per §168(k) as amended
PRE-OBBBA / TCJA phasedown still applies to property placed in service BEFORE 2025-01-19: TY2024 = 60%; TY2025 (pre-1/19) = 40%
Election OUT is by ASSET CLASS — applies to ALL bonus-eligible property in that class for the year
Form 4562 + statement attached to return (irrevocable once filed without IRS consent)
Income projection for future years (election worthless if future bracket isn't actually higher)
Coordinate with §179 expense election (separate decision — §179 is asset-by-asset)
strategies.action: Elect OUT of §168(k) bonus depreciation for TY{{taxYear}} via Form 4562 statement. Defer ${{bonusDep}} of deduction to higher-bracket future years. Bracket-arbitrage benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Adoption Credit §23
strategies.trigger: dependentsUnder17 >= 1 AND AGI < $292,150 (TY2024 phase-out top) AND no existing adoption_credit adjustment. CPA confirms actual adoption — engine cannot verify.
strategies.formula: Up to a per-child credit for qualified adoption expenses: $16,810 TY2024 (Notice 2023-75) / $17,280 TY2025 / $17,670 TY2026 (Rev. Proc. 2025-32). MAGI phase-out ($40k band): $252,150-$292,150 TY2024 / $259,190-$299,190 TY2025 / $265,080-$305,080 TY2026. Special-needs adoption: full credit regardless of expenses (§23(a)(3)). OBBBA (P.L. 119-21) makes up to $5,000 TY2025 / $5,120 TY2026 REFUNDABLE; the remainder is non-refundable with 5-year carryforward (§23(c)). estSavings = $5,000 typical (most parents NOT adopting — strong CPA-confirm caveat).
prerequisiteData: Confirm actual adoption occurred (or in process) — engine cannot verify; CPA confirms.
Qualified adoption expenses (Form 8839): adoption fees, attorney fees, court costs, travel — for adoption of a child under 18 or disabled adult
Special needs adoption: state-determined; full max credit ($16,810 TY2024 / $17,280 TY2025 / $17,670 TY2026) even without expenses
Non-refundable — capped at federal tax liability; 5-yr carryforward
MFS — disallowed (must file joint with spouse)
Foreign adoption — credit allowed only in year adoption finalized (Reg §1.23-1)
strategies.action: If client adopted (or is in process), claim Adoption Credit on Form 8839. Up to {{maxCredit}}/child. Heuristic typical $5,000 — but CPA confirms actual adoption + expenses.

strategies.name: Rollover-IRA → 401(k) — fix §408(d)(2) pro-rata trap for backdoor Roth
strategies.trigger: H5 traditional_ira balance > $1,000 AND AGI > Roth IRA contribution phase-out top (TY2024 $161k single / $240k MFJ) — backdoor Roth candidate (G1.26) with pro-rata trap.
strategies.formula: §408(d)(2) IRA aggregation includes ALL trad/SEP/SIMPLE IRAs at year-end but EXCLUDES 401(k) and other employer plans. Roll pre-tax IRA → current employer 401(k) BEFORE year-end, so backdoor Roth conversion is clean (100% tax-free). Without this, $7k backdoor + $100k pre-tax IRA → ~$6,542 taxable per §408(d)(2). estSavings = $7k × ratio × marginal = $1,540 at 22% on $100k trap.
prerequisiteData: Confirm 401(k) plan ACCEPTS incoming rollovers (most do, but plan-specific)
Verify plan-specific paperwork: rollover form, custodian-to-custodian direct transfer (recommended)
Determine pre-tax IRA balance to roll (Note: AFTER-TAX IRA basis CANNOT be rolled into 401(k); leave that portion for backdoor Roth conversion)
Time before backdoor Roth: roll IRA → 401(k) BEFORE year-end so it's not in the §408(d)(2) Dec-31 aggregation
Coordinate with G1.26 backdoor Roth (clean execution requires pre-tax IRA = $0)
Future-state consideration: 401(k) balance can be rolled back to IRA after leaving employer (round-trip flexibility)
NOT applicable if client has no 401(k) plan or self-employed (Solo 401(k) is an option then — see G1.1)
strategies.action: Roll {{rolloverAmount}} of pre-tax traditional IRA into current employer 401(k) BEFORE year-end. Cleans the §408(d)(2) aggregation for backdoor Roth (G1.26). Per-year backdoor Roth savings ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: NQDC §409A — Nonqualified Deferred Compensation election
strategies.trigger: W-2 wages > $400,000 (executive comp range) AND age between 40 and 55 (transition-to-retirement window) AND no existing NQDC adjustment marker.
strategies.formula: Defer current-year compensation beyond §401(a)(17) qualified plan limits via employer NQDC plan. Distribution at separation / fixed date / specified events per §409A(a)(2). Bracket arbitrage: defer at peak career marginal rate (35-37%); distribute in retirement at lower rate (22-24%). estSavings = $100,000 assumed deferral × (37% current − 22% retirement) = $15,000/yr.
prerequisiteData: Confirm employer offers an NQDC plan (most Fortune 500 + many startups do)
Initial election timing: must elect BEFORE start of service year per §409A(a)(4)(B). New-participant election within 30 days of plan eligibility.
Distribution events allowed (§409A(a)(2)): separation from service, death, disability, change in control, hardship, OR specified date/year
Distribution election locked in at deferral time — modification per §409A(a)(4)(C) requires 12-month delay + 5-year postponement
Violation penalty: immediate income recognition + 20% additional tax + interest at AFR + 1% per quarter (§409A(a)(1)(B))
Diversification + investment elections within NQDC plan (employer-tracked, NOT actually invested by employee)
Credit risk: NQDC is an unsecured promise — at risk if employer bankrupts. Diversify with other retirement vehicles.
Coordinate with golden-parachute payments under §280G (change-in-control deferrals)
strategies.action: If employer offers NQDC, elect to defer ~{{deferralAmount}} of next year's compensation. Distribute at separation in retirement at projected lower rate. Estimated annual bracket-arbitrage benefit ~{{estSavings}}. CRITICAL: elect BEFORE the year of service starts.

strategies.name: State residency change — relocate to no-tax state
strategies.ircSection: State law (CA Rev & Tax Code §17014; NY Tax Law §605; etc.)
strategies.irsPub: FTB Publication 1031 (CA); NY TSB-M-22(1)I; state DOR guidance
strategies.trigger: Resident state in [CA, NY, NJ, HI, OR, MA, CT, MD] (high-tax states) AND AGI > $500,000 AND state tax liability > $30,000 (material savings opportunity).
strategies.formula: Move domicile + statutory residence to no-tax state (TX, FL, NV, WA, TN, SD, AK, WY, NH partial). Heuristic: state tax saved = current state tax × 0.50 (assumed 50% effective relocation savings, accounting for partial-year + multi-state apportionment in transition year). HIGH-leverage for $500k+ AGI HNW filers but multi-year domicile establishment required.
prerequisiteData: Domicile vs residency tests vary by state. CA Rev & Tax Code §17014: 183-day test PLUS facts-and-circumstances domicile test.
Physical relocation + intent (driver's license, voter registration, primary residence purchase, doctor/dentist relocation, social ties)
Severance from former state (sell home or convert to investment, end memberships, close professional licenses where possible)
Note: NY 'statutory residence' test = (183+ days) AND (permanent place of abode) — even non-domiciliaries can be taxed
Trust + estate planning coordination — change trustee state, situs
Income sourcing: wages where earned, business income where business operated, rental income where property located (vs residence-based tax)
Audit risk: CA + NY known for aggressive residency challenges; document EVERYTHING (calendar, receipts, utility bills, family activity)
Mid-year move = part-year resident in both states (G1.E12 / Phase E covers part-year residency math)
strategies.action: Relocate domicile + residency from {{currentState}} to a no-tax state (TX, FL, NV, WA, TN, SD, AK, WY). Establish all facts of domicile + 183+ days in new state. Estimated annual state tax savings ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Coverdell Education Savings Account §530
strategies.trigger: AGI within phase-out ($95k-$110k single / $190k-$220k MFJ TY2024) AND dependentsUnder17 > 0 (qualifying child) AND no existing coverdell ESA adjustment.
strategies.formula: Contribute up to $2,000/yr per qualified child under 18 (or special needs to age 21). Tax-FREE growth + qualified K-12 + post-secondary education distributions. Distinct from §529 (which is K-12 limited to $10k/yr withdrawal). Heuristic estSavings (PV) = $2,000 × (1.07^15 − 1) × 0.22 / 1.05^15 = $686 per child.
prerequisiteData: Beneficiary under 18 (or special needs to age 21)
Contribution cap $2,000/yr per BENEFICIARY (not per contributor) — aggregate of all contributors
AGI phase-out TY2024: $95k-$110k single / $190k-$220k MFJ (Rev. Proc. 2023-34 §3.20)
Contribution deadline: April 15 of next year (same as IRA)
Distribution must be QUALIFIED education expense for K-12 OR post-secondary
Non-qualified withdrawals: earnings portion taxable + 10% penalty
Coordinate with §529 — both allowed; choose based on K-12 vs college focus
Rollover from Coverdell to §529 allowed (one-way per Rev. Proc. 2017-24)
strategies.action: Contribute $2,000/yr to a Coverdell ESA per qualifying child under 18. Tax-free K-12 + college growth. Long-term PV per child ~{{estSavings}} over 15-year horizon.

strategies.name: §41(h) R&D Credit Payroll-Tax Election (small biz)
strategies.trigger: Net SE between $100,000 and $5,000,000 (small biz gross-receipts proxy per §41(h)(3)(B)) AND has R&D activity signal (heuristic — tech/engineering SE) AND no existing R&D payroll election.
strategies.formula: Election allows qualified small business to apply R&D credit against EMPLOYER's payroll tax (FICA + Medicare side) up to $500k/yr (IRA 2022 doubled from $250k). Beneficial for pre-revenue startups with no income tax liability to absorb the credit. estSavings = $5,000 typical first-year benefit (small biz with $50k QREs × 6% ASC = $3k credit; payroll-tax monetization).
prerequisiteData: ELIGIBILITY (§41(h)(3)): qualified small business — gross receipts < $5M AND in business < 5 years (gross receipts must be < $5M in any of the 5 prior years to count)
Compute R&D credit per §41 (Alternative Simplified Method = 14% of current QRE − 50% of 3-yr avg, or 6% for first-time)
Election made on Form 6765 attached to timely-filed (incl extensions) return
Election applied to Form 8974 (Qualified Small Business Payroll Tax Credit for Increasing Research Activities) + Form 941 quarterly
Election is ANNUAL — re-elect each year
Coordinates with G1.36 (income-tax-side R&D credit): if income tax exists, take that side first; if not, payroll election fills the gap
Wages for §41 are W-2 wages PAID by the qualified small business — sole-prop SE earnings don't fund this (W-2 employee wages required)
strategies.action: If client is pre-revenue startup with R&D activity, file Form 6765 election (§41(h)) to apply R&D credit against employer payroll tax via Form 8974. Up to $500k/yr cap; typical first-year monetization ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: §221 Student Loan Interest Deduction
strategies.irsPub: Pub 970 Chapter 4; Form 1040 Sch 1 Line 21; Rev. Proc. 2023-34 §3.21
strategies.trigger: AGI within phase-out ($80k-$95k single / $165k-$195k MFJ TY2024) AND no existing student_loan_interest adjustment.
strategies.formula: Above-the-line deduction up to $2,500/yr of interest paid on qualified student loan. Phase-out: $80k-$95k single / $165k-$195k MFJ TY2024. Cannot claim if MFS, claimed as dependent, or interest paid on related-party loan. estSavings = min(actual interest paid, $2,500) × marginal rate. Typical $2,500 × 22% = $550.
prerequisiteData: Actual interest paid on qualified student loan in the tax year (Form 1098-E from servicer)
Loan must be qualified: incurred SOLELY to pay qualified higher education expenses for taxpayer / spouse / dependent
Eligible institutions: accredited post-secondary
AGI phase-out TY2024: $80k-$95k single / $165k-$195k MFJ (Rev. Proc. 2023-34 §3.21)
MFS — DISQUALIFIED (cannot claim)
Dependent of another taxpayer — DISQUALIFIED
Related-party loans (from family member) — DISQUALIFIED
Phase-out reduction: ratable based on (AGI − floor) / $15k single or $30k MFJ
strategies.action: Deduct up to $2,500 of student loan interest paid this year on Schedule 1 Line 21. Above-the-line — reduces AGI. Saves ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Estimated Tax Safe Harbor §6654 — avoid underpayment penalty
strategies.trigger: Net SE > $20,000 AND federal tax liability > $5,000 AND insufficient withholding signal (no explicit withholding tracking — heuristic on SE-heavy income mix).
strategies.formula: Safe harbor (§6654(d)(1)(B)): pay quarterly estimated tax = greater of (a) 100% of PRIOR year's total tax (110% if AGI > $150k single, $75k MFS), OR (b) 90% of CURRENT year's tax. Penalty rate = AFR + 3% applied to underpayment by quarter. estSavings = typical penalty avoidance ~$300.
prerequisiteData: Prior year's TOTAL TAX (line 24 Form 1040) for the 100/110% calc
Year-to-date withholding (W-2 box 2 + 1099 withholding) for the shortfall calc
Quarterly income estimate to schedule payments per §6654(c) (Apr 15 / Jun 15 / Sep 15 / Jan 15)
Optional: annualized-income method via Form 2210 Schedule AI for clients with uneven income (e.g., seasonal SE)
Note: 110% threshold ($150k AGI single / $75k MFS) — applies based on PRIOR year AGI
Withholding is treated as paid evenly throughout the year; estimated tax payments are credited by quarter
strategies.action: Compute the §6654 safe harbor for TY{{taxYear}}. Pay at least {{safeHarborPct}}% of prior year tax in quarterly installments. Avoids typical penalty ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Kiddie Tax §1(g) — minimize child's unearned income exposure
strategies.trigger: dependentsUnder17 > 0 AND AGI > $200,000 (HNW proxy — likely funding UGMA/UTMA accounts) AND no existing kiddie-tax adjustment.
strategies.formula: TY2024: child's unearned income > $2,600 ($1,300 free + $1,300 child's rate) taxed at PARENT's marginal rate via Form 8615. Planning: shift child's investments to growth-oriented (LTCG-favored at sale) OR tax-deferred wrappers (529, Roth IRA) OR delay realization until child turns 18 (kiddie-tax sunset). estSavings = ~$5,000 typical excess unearned income × (parent 32% − child 10%) = $1,100/yr per affected child.
prerequisiteData: Child's age (kiddie tax applies under 18; OR 18-23 if full-time student dependent; OR 18 if no earned income > half support)
Child's unearned income (interest, dividends, capital gains in their name)
Current investment allocation in UGMA / UTMA accounts
Whether child can be made an UTMA-to-self transfer at age 18-25 (state-dependent)
Parent's marginal rate (drives the rate differential)
Form 8615 required if child has unearned income > $2,600 AND files own return; OR Form 8814 if parent elects to report on parent's return
strategies.action: For dependent children under 18 (or 18-23 student) with unearned income > $2,600, shift to growth-oriented investments (LTCG-favored) or tax-deferred wrappers. Per affected child, saves ~{{estSavings}}/year of parent-rate-on-child-income.

strategies.name: §183 Hobby Loss qualification — preserve business loss deductibility
strategies.trigger: Net SE income positive but small ($1,000 - $10,000 range, signals hobby concern — profit-motive may not satisfy §183(d) safe harbor) AND no clear documentation flag.
strategies.formula: If activity classified as HOBBY (not business): post-TCJA NO expense deduction allowed (was 2%-AGI misc itemized pre-2018). Loss recovery only if activity is genuine business. Safe harbor §183(d): presumed for profit if profitable 3 of 5 years (2 of 7 for horse-breeding). 9-factor test per Reg §1.183-2(b). estSavings = $5,000 typical loss recovery × marginal rate (e.g., $5k × 24% = $1,200).
prerequisiteData: Profit history: profitable in how many of last 5 years? (3-of-5 safe harbor)
Documented business plan + profit motive
Time + effort devoted (full-time vs sporadic)
Expertise of taxpayer or advisors (formal training, certifications)
Expectation of asset appreciation (e.g., land, livestock breeding)
Prior success in similar / dissimilar activities
Income / loss trend pattern (gradual ramp vs persistent loss)
Personal pleasure factor (less is more)
Detailed contemporaneous records — separate bank account, accounting books, written contracts
strategies.action: Document profit motive per Reg §1.183-2(b) 9-factor test. Aim for 3-of-5-years profitability safe harbor under §183(d). Properly documented activity preserves ~{{estSavings}} of loss deductibility per year.

strategies.name: Custodial Roth IRA for child — 50+ years of tax-free growth
strategies.trigger: Net SE > $50,000 AND dependentsUnder17 > 0 (proxy for parent employing kids via G1.49 — children would have earned income to fund Roth IRA). Pairs with G1.49.
strategies.formula: Child with earned income (W-2 from family business or other employer) can fund custodial Roth IRA up to lesser of $7,000 (TY2024) or earned income. ZERO current-year tax benefit (Roth is after-tax). MASSIVE long-term benefit: 50+ years of tax-free growth. estSavings (PV) = $7,000 × (1.07^50 - 1) × 0.32 / 1.05^50. PV ≈ $2,422 per year contributed.
prerequisiteData: Child has earned income — W-2 wages or self-employment net (NOT investment income, gifts, allowances)
Contribution cap: lesser of $7,000 (TY2024) or child's earned income
Custodial Roth IRA opened (parent or guardian as custodian; child takes control at age 18 or 21 by state)
Documentation: child's W-2 from parent's business (G1.49 setup) OR other employer
Parent CANNOT claim child as dependent if child's wages exceed half of own support — usually moot until child has substantial wages
Child must file own return if earned income > $14,600 (TY2024 std ded)
Roth contributions ALWAYS withdrawable tax-free (basis recovery rule per Treas. Reg. §1.408A-6 Q&A 8)
strategies.action: Open a custodial Roth IRA for each child with earned income (from G1.49 family employment or other source). Contribute up to $7,000/child or earned income, whichever less. Long-term PV ~{{estSavings}}/child/year given 50+ year horizon.

strategies.name: Specific-Share-ID lot selection at sale (HIFO / specific lots)
strategies.trigger: LTCG OR STCG > $5,000 from 1099-B (brokerage signal) AND no existing specific-share-ID flag.
strategies.formula: Default lot identification at sale = FIFO (first-in-first-out). Election to use SPECIFIC-share-ID (e.g., HIFO highest-in-first-out, or pick specific lots) per Treas. Reg. §1.1012-1(c)(7)(ii). Election must be made AT TIME OF SALE — not retroactively. estSavings = (highest_lot_basis − FIFO_basis) × LTCG rate. Heuristic: assume 20% basis differential × LTCG 20% = 4% of gain.
prerequisiteData: Per-lot cost basis records from broker (1099-B shows by lot or aggregate depending on broker)
Holding period of each lot (LT vs ST — substantial rate differential)
Decision at TIME OF SALE (not 1040 filing) — must instruct broker which specific shares to sell BEFORE the trade settles
Once chosen, lot designation generally cannot be changed
Note: mutual funds default = average cost; can elect specific-ID for non-covered shares (acquired before 2012 for stocks)
Covered shares post-2012: specific-ID election allowed per IRC §6045(g)(2)(B)
strategies.action: Before placing the sell order, instruct broker to use SPECIFIC LOT identification (HIFO or specific). Saves ~{{estSavings}} on ${{saleAmount}} of partial sale (4%-of-gain heuristic).

strategies.name: ACA Premium Tax Credit §36B — MAGI projection + reconciliation
strategies.trigger: AGI within ACA-relevant range ($30k-$120k household, roughly 100-400% FPL TY2024) AND has self-employment income (proxy for non-employer coverage) AND no existing premium_tax_credit adjustment.
strategies.formula: Project current-year MAGI accurately mid-year. If MAGI ended > projected: client may owe back portion of advance credit (capped per §36B(f)(2)(B) repayment cap for households < 400% FPL — uncapped at 400%+). Heuristic estSavings = ~$1,000 (rough avg overpayment avoidance) when MAGI projection is sharp.
prerequisiteData: Confirm client has marketplace QHP coverage (vs employer-sponsored coverage which disqualifies)
1095-A (Marketplace Health Insurance Statement) — provides advance PTC + actual premiums
Estimated MAGI for the year (AGI + tax-exempt interest + foreign income + excluded SS)
Household composition (changes mid-year affect FPL calc)
Mid-year income changes (raise, bonus, business pivot) — contact Marketplace within 30 days to update
FPL ceiling: post-IRA 2022 + §80101 extended ZERO cap through 2025 (no 400% cliff for TY2021-2025); 400% cliff REINSTATES TY2026 absent legislation
strategies.action: If client is on Marketplace coverage, refine current-year MAGI projection. For TY{{taxYear}} (post-IRA 2022 cap removed through 2025), repayment risk is reduced. Avg overpayment-avoidance benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: §1045 QSBS Rollover — defer gain into another QSBS (60-day window)
strategies.trigger: Founder-profile signal: K-1 active > $50k OR net SE > $200k OR total income > $200k. AND LTCG > $500,000. AND no existing section_1045_rollover marker.
strategies.formula: Roll gain from QSBS sale into another QSBS within 60 days of sale. Basis carryover to new QSBS; gain DEFERRED (not eliminated). Original holding-period requirement: held > 6 months at sale. estSavings = deferred gain × (20% LTCG + 3.8% NIIT) × 0.3 time-value factor.
prerequisiteData: Confirm sold stock was QUALIFIED §1202 QSBS at sale (all 6 §1202 requirements)
Confirm original stock held > 6 months (vs §1202's > 5 years for outright exclusion)
Identify replacement QSBS within 60 days of sale (strict deadline)
Replacement stock must ALSO qualify as §1202 QSBS (corp gross assets ≤ $50M at issuance, etc.)
Investment must be in QSBS within the 60-day window — cash held outside qualifies only if rolled by deadline
Filed via Form 8949 with code 'R' for rollover
Reduces basis of replacement stock by deferred gain amount
strategies.action: If client sold qualified §1202 QSBS held > 6 months and reinvested in new QSBS within 60 days, file §1045 rollover on Form 8949 (code R). Defers ~{{estSavings}} of LTCG + NIIT.

strategies.name: Self-Employed Health Insurance §162(l) — 100% above-the-line
strategies.trigger: Net SE earnings > $30,000 AND no existing self_employed_health_insurance_premiums adjustment AND client buys own health insurance (NOT eligible for spouse-employer-sponsored coverage).
strategies.formula: 100% deduction of qualified health insurance premiums (medical / dental / vision / long-term care) for the SE filer + spouse + dependents. Capped at (net SE − half-SE-tax deduction − qualified retirement contribution) per §162(l)(2)(A). Above-the-line on Schedule 1 Line 17. estSavings = assumed $12,000/yr premiums × (federal + state marginal rate).
prerequisiteData: Confirm SE filer is NOT eligible to participate in employer-subsidized health plan via OWN or SPOUSE'S employer per §162(l)(2)(B)
Premium amount paid for the year (medical + dental + vision + qualified LTC)
Cap: deduction can't exceed (net SE − half-SE deduction − retirement plan contributions) per §162(l)(2)(A)
Coordinate with §36B PTC (G1.30) — SE filer with ACA coverage gets both subsidy AND deduction with circular calc (Pub 974 iterative method)
S-corp owners: premiums must be reported on W-2 box 1 to claim §162(l) (S-corp owns the policy + reimburses or pays)
strategies.action: Deduct ${{premiumsAssumed}} of self-employed health insurance premiums above-the-line on Schedule 1 Line 17. Saves ~{{estSavings}} at the combined marginal rate.

strategies.name: Wash-Sale Proactive Avoidance §1091 (forward-looking)
strategies.trigger: Has capital_loss_carryforward_short + _long > $5,000 (signal of active TLH) AND no existing wash-sale flag.
strategies.formula: Forward-looking: when realizing a capital loss for tax purposes, do NOT repurchase substantially-identical security within 30 days before OR after the sale (61-day window). Wash-sale disallows the loss + tacks basis onto the replacement. estSavings = $3,000 typical forfeit avoided × marginal ordinary rate (loss would have offset ordinary up to §1211 cap). Heuristic: $3,000 × 22% = $660.
prerequisiteData: List of holdings client plans to sell for tax loss
List of any pending purchases of those (or substantially-identical) securities
61-day window awareness: 30 days BEFORE + sale date + 30 days AFTER
Substantially-identical determination: same stock = yes; bond/preferred from same issuer = situational; ETF tracking same index = often yes; sector ETF rotation = usually safe
Cross-account wash sale: spouse's IRA / brokerage / Roth IRA — IRS Rev. Rul. 2008-5 confirms IRA-side wash sale (no basis adjustment available → permanent forfeit)
Engine already auto-detects wash sales via E13 (post-event); this strategy is PRE-event coaching
strategies.action: Before client realizes capital losses, review pending buys to prevent §1091 wash-sale. Avoid repurchase within 30 days. Estimated prevented forfeit ~{{estSavings}} on typical TLH cycle.

strategies.name: §72(t) SEPP — penalty-free IRA withdrawal before 59½
strategies.trigger: taxpayerAge between 50 and 58 AND H5 traditional/SEP/SIMPLE IRA total balance > $200,000 AND total income < $200,000 (proxy for early-retirement transition vs accumulation phase).
strategies.formula: Substantially Equal Periodic Payments — withdraw fixed amount annually from IRA without 10% §72(t) penalty. Must continue 5 yrs OR until age 59½, whichever is later. 3 methods per Notice 2022-6: (1) RMD method, (2) fixed amortization, (3) fixed annuitization. Heuristic: avoid 10% penalty on $30k/yr × 5 yrs = $15,000.
prerequisiteData: Confirm client intends to retire / withdraw before age 59½ (else no benefit vs simple withdrawal)
Choose ONE method (RMD / amortization / annuitization) per Notice 2022-6 — election is locked in for the SEPP period
Interest rate cap: not more than 120% of mid-term Applicable Federal Rate (AFR) for the 2 prior months
Account isolation: typically establish dedicated SEPP IRA via partial rollover (so only that account follows the SEPP rules)
Continue 5 yrs OR to 59½ — whichever LATER. Modification before that = 10% penalty + interest on ALL prior years' SEPP withdrawals retroactively
Required Beginning Date for RMDs (age 73 SECURE 2.0) applies separately — coordinate
strategies.action: Establish a §72(t) SEPP from a dedicated IRA. Withdraw ~$30,000/year using fixed amortization method. Saves the 10% early-withdrawal penalty (~{{estSavings}} over 5 years). Carefully avoid modification — penalty is RETROACTIVE on all prior years.

strategies.name: Spousal IRA — non-working spouse contribution §219(c)
strategies.trigger: Filing status MFJ AND total earned income (W-2 + net SE) > $7,000 AND no existing spouse_ira_contribution adjustment. Heuristic: applies when CPA confirms one spouse has $0 earned income (engine cannot verify per-spouse split).
strategies.formula: Non-working spouse contributes up to $7,000 ($8,000 50+) to traditional IRA using working spouse's earned income. Deductible above-the-line (subject to retirement-plan coverage phase-out per §219(g)). estSavings = $7,000 × (federal + state marginal rate).
prerequisiteData: Confirm filing status MFJ (single + MFS NOT eligible)
Confirm non-working spouse's earned income for the year ($0 to claim full spousal benefit)
Working spouse's earned income must equal or exceed total IRA contributions (working + spousal)
If working spouse is covered by a retirement plan, deduction phases out for MFJ at $230k-$240k (TY2024) for the SPOUSAL contribution (different from active-participant phase-out at $123k-$143k)
Spousal Roth IRA option also available (subject to direct-Roth phase-out)
strategies.action: If client is MFJ and one spouse has limited / no earned income, contribute up to $7,000 ($8k 50+) to a spousal Traditional or Roth IRA. Traditional saves ~{{estSavings}} on current return; Roth gives tax-free future growth.

strategies.name: §453 Installment Sale — defer large gain over years
strategies.trigger: H5 real_estate OR primary_residence asset with embedded gain (balance − costBasis) > $250,000 AND AGI > $250,000 (good candidate for bracket-smoothing) AND no existing installment_sale election.
strategies.formula: Recognize gain pro-rata as payments received: gross_profit / contract_price × payment_each_year. Smoothes a large one-year gain across multiple years → lower blended marginal rate + potential NIIT avoidance. estSavings = gain × 5% timing-benefit factor (conservative; assumes ~20% NIIT/marginal differential applied to ~25% of gain spread across 5 yrs).
prerequisiteData: Confirm sale of property type qualifies (real property + most personal property OK; PUBLICLY-TRADED SECURITIES + DEALER DISPOSITIONS do NOT qualify per §453(b)(2))
Confirm at least one payment received AFTER close of taxable year (Form 6252 required)
Estimate gross profit, contract price, gross profit ratio for each tax year
Depreciation recapture (§1250) is RECOGNIZED IN YEAR OF SALE — only excess flows through installment method
If interest is imputed (§483 / §1274), interest portion is ordinary income separately
Election to OPT OUT exists — file by due date of return if buyer-credit concern
strategies.action: Structure the sale as an installment with payments over {{plannedYears}} years. Recognize ~{{annualGain}}/year instead of {{totalGain}} in a single year. Heuristic timing benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: §83(b) election timing — pre-vest income recognition
strategies.trigger: H5 restricted_stock_pre_83b asset (NEW asset type — implies client received restricted stock + did NOT make §83(b) election). Engine flags the strategy for FUTURE grants (current grant past the 30-day deadline).
strategies.formula: WITHOUT §83(b): include FMV at each vest as ordinary W-2 income. WITH §83(b) filed within 30 days of grant: include FMV at GRANT (typically much lower) as ordinary income now; subsequent appreciation = capital gain. estSavings = balance × 30% assumed appreciation × (37% ordinary − 20% LTCG) = balance × 5.1%.
prerequisiteData: Strict 30-DAY DEADLINE from GRANT date (NOT vest date) — see Reg. §1.83-2(a)
Election form filed with IRS service center (NO electronic filing) + copy to employer + attach to next return
Election is IRREVOCABLE — risk: if equity becomes worthless before vest, the tax paid on FMV-at-grant is LOST (no refund)
Best fit: early-stage startup founders (FMV at grant = very low or $0) with strong upside expectation
Worst fit: late-stage pre-IPO grants where FMV is already high — pay big tax now without certainty of future gain
strategies.action: For NEW restricted stock grants (NOT current — past the deadline), evaluate §83(b) within 30 days. Likely benefit on grants with low FMV-at-grant + high upside ~{{estSavings}}. CAUTION: irrevocable + risk of paying tax on worthless equity.

strategies.name: Family Employment of Children §3121(b)(3)(A) (FICA-exempt wages)
strategies.trigger: Has self-employment income > $50,000 (sole prop / single-member LLC proxy) AND dependentsUnder17 > 0 (proxy for kids who could work) AND no existing family_employment marker.
strategies.formula: Sole prop (or husband-wife partnership) can pay child under 18 wages that are EXEMPT from FICA + FUTA (until 21). Wages deductible to business; child's standard deduction shields $14,600 (TY2024) from federal income tax. estSavings = $14,600 × (parent marginal + 7.65% SE FICA × 2 employer+employee) per child × 1 child assumed.
prerequisiteData: Business MUST be SOLE PROP or husband-wife PARTNERSHIP — S-CORP or C-CORP wages to children ARE subject to FICA
Child must actually PERFORM real work + receive reasonable wages (no sham employment)
Document: timesheets, job description, W-2 issued, payroll records
Child under 18: FICA + Medicare exempt; under 21: FUTA exempt (§3306(c)(5))
Child's standard deduction $14,600 TY2024 shields wages from federal income tax
Child can also fund Roth IRA up to earned income (powerful long-term)
State income tax + state SUTA may still apply — check state law
strategies.action: Hire eligible child(ren) under 18 to perform real work in sole-prop business. Pay up to $14,600/child = FICA-exempt + parent deducts at marginal rate. Estimated savings ~{{estSavings}}. Document carefully.

strategies.name: AOC vs LLC choice §25A (use AOC for undergrads when available)
strategies.trigger: Has qualified_education_expenses_llc > 0 AND no qualified_education_expenses_aoc AND AGI within phase-out ($90k single / $180k MFJ ceiling). Suggests client may be using LLC for an undergrad student who's AOC-eligible.
strategies.formula: AOC: $2,500 max per student (100% of first $2k + 25% of next $2k); 4 yrs post-secondary; 40% refundable. LLC: $2,000 max per RETURN (20% of first $10k); unlimited yrs; non-refundable. SWITCHING from LLC to AOC for an undergrad student picks up $500 extra credit (assuming same $4k qualified expenses). estSavings = $500.
prerequisiteData: Student must be enrolled at least half-time, working toward degree, in first 4 years of post-secondary education (AOC requirements)
Has not claimed AOC for this student in 4 prior tax years
Has not been convicted of a felony drug offense
Form 1098-T from eligible educational institution
Phase-out: AOC $80k-$90k single / $160k-$180k MFJ (TY2024); LLC has identical phase-out
Each student can only get ONE of AOC or LLC per year (NOT both)
strategies.action: If client's LLC-claimed student is an undergrad (first 4 yrs), switch to American Opportunity Credit on Form 8863. Saves ~{{estSavings}} ($2,500 AOC vs $2,000 LLC ceiling) per eligible student per year for up to 4 yrs.

strategies.name: §529 → Roth IRA rollover (SECURE 2.0)
strategies.trigger: H5 529 asset balance >= $35,000 (lifetime rollover cap). CPA confirms 529 has been open >= 15 years AND contributions being rolled are >= 5 years old.
strategies.formula: Annual rollover up to IRA cap ($7k TY2024 under 50; $8k 50+); lifetime cap $35,000 per beneficiary. Treated as Roth IRA contribution — counts against the annual IRA limit. estSavings (PV) = $35k × (1.07^20 − 1) × 0.32 / 1.05^20 = ~$12,114 long-term Roth growth.
prerequisiteData: Date 529 account was opened (must be 15+ years old)
Date of contributions being rolled (must be at least 5 years old per §529(c)(3)(E)(iii))
Beneficiary must have earned income equal to the rollover amount in the year
529 beneficiary owns the Roth IRA (beneficiary = recipient)
Lifetime $35k cap is per BENEFICIARY (not per account)
Annual rollover counts against the IRA contribution limit ($7k / $8k)
strategies.action: Roll up to $7,000/year ($8k if 50+) from the 529 to the beneficiary's Roth IRA. Lifetime cap $35,000. Long-term PV of tax-free Roth growth ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Saver's Credit §25B (Retirement Savings Contributions)
strategies.trigger: AGI within phase-out (TY2024 single: < $38,250; MFJ: < $76,500; HoH: < $57,375) AND has any retirement contribution (IRA / 401(k) / 403(b) / etc.) AND no existing retirement_contributions_savers adjustment AND age >= 18 AND not a full-time student/dependent.
strategies.formula: Credit = applicable_rate × min(contributions, $2,000 single / $4,000 MFJ). Rate brackets TY2024: 50% (single < $23k / MFJ < $46k); 20% (single $23,001-$25,000 / MFJ $46k-$50,000); 10% (single $25,001-$38,250 / MFJ $50k-$76,500); 0% above. Non-refundable.
prerequisiteData: Confirm client age >= 18
Confirm client is NOT a full-time student (5+ months) and NOT claimed as dependent
Total retirement contributions for the year (IRA / 401(k) employee deferral / 403(b) / Solo 401(k))
AGI computation (used to determine bracket)
Note: existing IRA rollovers + distributions reduce the qualifying contribution amount (§25B(d)(2))
strategies.action: If client is contributing to retirement and AGI is within the Saver's Credit phase-out, claim ~{{estSavings}} on Form 8880. Non-refundable — usable up to federal tax liability.

strategies.name: Dependent Care FSA vs §21 Credit choice
strategies.trigger: Has dependent_care_expenses > 0 AND has W-2 income (eligible for employer DCFSA enrollment) AND federal marginal rate >= 22% (DCFSA beats §21 credit at higher brackets).
strategies.formula: DCFSA: $5,000 elective deferral pre-tax. Saves marginal (federal + state) tax + FICA 7.65%. §21 credit: 20-35% of $3k/1 child or $6k/2+ children depending on AGI. Heuristic estSavings = $5,000 × (marginal + 0.0765) − ($600 §21 credit already claimed for $3k expense at 20% rate).
prerequisiteData: Confirm employer offers DCFSA enrollment (most do; verify with HR)
Number of qualifying children + qualifying expenses
Marriage / earned-income status of both spouses (DCFSA requires both work, §21 same)
Coordinate with §21 credit cap: DCFSA election dollars REDUCE the §21 qualifying expenses (no double-dip)
strategies.action: For TY{{nextTaxYear}}, elect ${{dcfsaAmount}} of pre-tax DCFSA via employer payroll. Saves ~{{estSavings}}/yr vs taking the §21 credit alone. Strategy is FORWARD-looking — must elect during open enrollment.

strategies.name: R&D Tax Credit §41 (Alternative Simplified Method)
strategies.trigger: Net SE earnings > $100,000 (proxy for tech / engineering / software dev profile) AND no existing R&D credit adjustment.
strategies.formula: Alternative Simplified Credit (ASC) = 14% × (current-year QREs − 50% of prior-3-yr avg QREs). For first-time claimants: 6% × current QREs. Heuristic estSavings = $50,000 assumed QREs × 14% = $7,000. Larger when QREs scale.
prerequisiteData: Identify Qualified Research Expenses (QREs): wages for R&D activities, supplies, 65% of contract research
Four-part test (§41(d)): permitted purpose, technological in nature, elimination of uncertainty, process of experimentation
Track time studies / project documentation per Reg. §1.41-4(c)
Form 6765 required (election: regular credit OR alternative simplified method)
§174 capitalization (post-TCJA): R&D expenses MUST be capitalized + amortized over 5 yrs (15 yrs foreign); credit reduces capitalizable basis
Small business (gross receipts < $5M, in business < 5 yrs): can use credit against payroll tax up to $500k/yr
Most state credits also available (CA, MA, NY, AZ, MN — separate filings)
strategies.action: If client performs qualifying R&D (software development, engineering, scientific experimentation), file Form 6765. On $50k QREs, ASC credit ~{{estSavings}}. Setup requires QRE documentation + 4-part test analysis.

strategies.name: Energy Efficient Home Improvement Credit §25C (heat pumps / windows / doors)
strategies.trigger: OBBBA (P.L. 119-21): §25C credit TERMINATED for property placed in service after 12/31/2025 — TY2025 only, UNAVAILABLE TY2026+. Client owns home (H5 primary_residence OR mortgage_interest > 0) AND no existing energy_efficient_home or energy_efficient_heatpump adjustment AND federal tax liability >= $1,000.
strategies.formula: 30% credit on qualifying improvements. Annual caps: $1,200 general; $2,000 heat pumps / heat pump water heaters / biomass; $600 windows; $250 per exterior door (max $500); $150 home energy audit; $600 central AC / furnace / boiler. Heuristic estSavings = $5,000 heat pump install × 30% = $1,500.
prerequisiteData: Identify qualifying ENERGY STAR improvements: heat pump, heat pump water heater, central AC, furnace, biomass stove, insulation, windows, doors
Manufacturer's qualifying certification (Notice 2024-09)
Receipts + installation date
ANNUAL cap — separate from §25D (residential clean energy 30% with no cap)
NO carryforward — use it or lose it each year (vs §25D which carries forward)
Rental property does NOT qualify
Installed home energy audit by qualified auditor needed for $150 credit
strategies.action: Install ENERGY STAR heat pump / windows / insulation at primary residence. TY{{taxYear}} 30% credit (~{{estSavings}} on $5k heat pump). Annual cap $3,200 combined ($1,200 + $2,000 heat pump). NO carryforward — replenishes each year.

strategies.name: §1244 Ordinary Loss on Small Business Stock
strategies.trigger: Capital loss carryforward (ST + LT) > $25,000 AND no §1244 marker AND total income > $100,000 (loss has material recovery value).
strategies.formula: §1244 lets up to $50,000 single / $100,000 MFJ of loss on qualifying small business stock be ORDINARY (deductible without the $3k cap) instead of capital (limited to $3k/yr against ordinary). Spread: ordinary marginal rate (24-37%) − LTCG rate (0-20%) = up to 17 percentage points of recovery. Heuristic estSavings = $50k × 0.17 = $8,500 (single).
prerequisiteData: Stock must be DOMESTIC C-CORP (not S-corp, not LLC, not partnership)
At time of issuance: corporation must have raised ≤ $1,000,000 in equity (small business plan-of-financing requirement)
Stock issued for MONEY or PROPERTY (not services)
Original-issuance: client must be the ORIGINAL holder (not secondary purchaser)
Corp's gross receipts > 50% from active trade/business in 5 yrs preceding loss (NOT investment income, holding cos, etc.)
Loss must be from sale, exchange, or worthlessness (Form 4797 Part I, NOT Sch D)
Annual ordinary-loss cap: $50k single / MFS, $100k MFJ. Excess flows to capital loss.
strategies.action: If client has worthless or sold-at-loss stock from a failed small business C-corp, verify §1244 qualifying-stock criteria. Up to {{ordinaryCap}} of the loss converts from capital (capped at $3k/yr) to ordinary (full deduction). Estimated recovery ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Inherited IRA — 10-year distribution rule (SECURE 1.0)
strategies.trigger: H5 traditional_ira balance > $50,000 AND taxpayerAge < 60 (likely inheritance-recipient profile). CPA confirms inheritance + decedent's relationship.
strategies.formula: 10-year distribution timing: spread distributions across low-income years instead of bunching. estSavings = balance × marginal × 0.05 (heuristic timing benefit of ~5% by avoiding bracket creep). Annual RMDs also required years 1-9 if decedent died after their RBD (Required Beginning Date) per Final Reg §1.401(a)(9)-5.
prerequisiteData: Is this an inherited IRA from a NON-SPOUSE decedent who died after 2019-12-31? (Spouse beneficiaries can still treat as own / use lifetime stretch.)
Date of decedent's death (drives the 10-year clock start)
Whether decedent was already taking RMDs at death (RBD) — drives whether year 1-9 RMDs are required IN ADDITION to the year-10 full distribution
Beneficiary's expected income trajectory through year 10 (bracket arbitrage window)
Whether the IRA is segregated or commingled (commingling disqualifies inherited treatment)
strategies.action: If this is an inherited IRA from a non-spouse decedent post-2019, plan distribution timing across the 10-year window. Front-load distributions in lowest-bracket years; potentially convert pre-RMD years to Roth via this 10-year window. Estimated timing benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Defined Benefit / Cash Balance Plan (for high-income SE)
strategies.trigger: Net SE earnings >= $300,000 AND taxpayerAge >= 45 AND no existing self-employed-retirement contribution >= $69k (signal client isn't already at DB-style cap).
strategies.formula: DB/CB plans allow age-actuarial contributions far above §415(c) DC cap. Heuristic by age band: 45-49 = $150k; 50-54 = $200k; 55-59 = $250k; 60+ = $300k. estSavings = min(contribution, netSE × 0.5) × (federal + state marginal rate). Plan also stackable with a Solo 401(k) for incremental defined-contribution side.
prerequisiteData: Engagement with a third-party actuary to design the DB/CB plan (~$3-5k setup; ~$2-3k/yr maintenance)
Plan sponsor election: DB (defined benefit), CB (cash balance hybrid), or DB+401(k) combined
Funding commitment: DB plans REQUIRE annual funding even in low-income years (vs DC plans which are discretionary). Plan to fund consistently.
Census of all employees (if any) — DB plans must satisfy nondiscrimination + may require contributions for staff
Form 5500 + actuarial certification filing each year
strategies.action: For high-income SE clients age 45+, design a Defined Benefit (or Cash Balance) plan with an actuary. Annual contribution by age band ~{{contribution}}; tax savings ~{{estSavings}}. Setup cost ~$5k; recurring CPA + actuary ~$3-5k/yr — pays back ~100x.

strategies.name: Clean Vehicle Credit §30D (new EV) / §25E (used EV)
strategies.trigger: OBBBA (P.L. 119-21): §30D/§25E clean-vehicle credit TERMINATED for vehicles acquired after 9/30/2025 — TY2025 only (Jan–Sep), UNAVAILABLE TY2026+. AGI <= $300,000 MFJ / $150,000 single (eligibility cap) AND no existing EV credit on the return.
strategies.formula: §30D New EV credit: up to $7,500 ($3,750 critical-minerals + $3,750 battery components). §25E Used EV: up to $4,000 or 30% of price (max $25k vehicle). MAGI limits — point-of-sale transfer to dealer also available 2024+.
prerequisiteData: OBBBA cutoff: vehicle ACQUIRED on or before 2025-09-30 (binding contract + payment by that date; placed-in-service may be later per the IRS OBBB FAQ)
Confirm vehicle is on the qualified list at fueleconomy.gov (manufacturer-specific; many EVs DO NOT qualify due to battery/critical-mineral sourcing rules)
MAGI test: $150k single / $300k MFJ / $225k HoH for §30D (use prior or current year, whichever is lower)
MSRP cap: $80k SUV/truck/van; $55k cars
Point-of-sale dealer transfer or claim on Form 8936
Used EV §25E: vehicle 2+ model years old; price <= $25k; sold by a dealer (not private party); same 9/30/2025 acquisition cutoff
strategies.action: Client is income-eligible for the Clean Vehicle Credit — but OBBBA TERMINATED §30D/§25E for vehicles acquired after 9/30/2025. For a TY2025 return: confirm the qualifying EV was ACQUIRED (binding contract + payment) on or before 2025-09-30, then claim ~{{estSavings}} on the TY{{taxYear}} return via Form 8936 (point-of-sale transfers reconcile there too). No acquisitions qualify after that date.

strategies.name: Residential Clean Energy Credit §25D (solar / battery / geothermal)
strategies.trigger: OBBBA (P.L. 119-21): §25D residential clean-energy credit TERMINATED for expenditures after 12/31/2025 — TY2025 only, UNAVAILABLE TY2026+. Client owns home (H5 primary_residence OR mortgage_interest adjustment) AND no existing residential_clean_energy adjustment AND AGI > $50k (sufficient tax liability to use the non-refundable credit, though carryforward exists).
strategies.formula: 30% credit on qualified residential solar / battery / geothermal / small-wind / fuel-cell installations through 2032 (phases to 26% in 2033, 22% in 2034, expires after 2034). estSavings = $20,000 assumed install × 30% = $6,000. Indefinite carryforward of unused credit. NO income cap.
prerequisiteData: Confirm equipment installed at primary or secondary RESIDENCE (no rental property)
Manufacturer certification + invoice (Notice 2013-70 documentation)
Battery storage: 3+ kWh capacity required for §25D
Geothermal: ENERGY STAR certified
Solar PV: includes structural mounting + permitting; CPA confirms labor + installation included in basis
Form 5695 + receipts; depreciation NOT taken (residence)
strategies.action: If client is considering solar / battery / geothermal install at primary residence, claim 30% credit (~{{estSavings}} on a $20k install) via Form 5695. Through 2032 at 30% — phases down 2033-2034. Indefinite carryforward of unused credit.

strategies.name: §1202 QSBS — OBBBA-expanded $75M gross assets / $15M cap / tiered hold
strategies.irsPub: Pub 550; Form 8949 (code Q); Rev. Rul. 2009-9; OBBBA §70432
strategies.trigger: AGI > $500,000 AND has K-1 active ordinary income (proxy for closely-held business owner / founder profile) AND no existing qsbs_gross_gain adjustment.
strategies.formula: POST-OBBBA (stock issued AFTER 2025-07-04): aggregate gross assets cap raised to $75M (indexed from 2027); per-issuer gain cap raised to $15M (indexed from 2027). NEW TIERED HOLDING for post-OBBBA stock: 50% exclusion at 3 yrs / 75% at 4 yrs / 100% at 5 yrs (vs old binary 5-yr 100%). NOTE: includible gain at 50%/75% tiers taxed at 28% rate (vs standard LTCG 15-20%). PRE-OBBBA stock (issued before 2025-07-04): $50M aggregate gross assets cap / $10M per-issuer / 5-yr binary 100% exclusion remains. Heuristic estSavings = $1M assumed qualifying gain × (20% LTCG + 3.8% NIIT) = $238k (one-time, on eventual sale).
prerequisiteData: Confirm stock is C-corp (NOT LLC, S-corp, or partnership)
Stock issuance date determines applicable rules: PRE-OBBBA (≤ 2025-07-04) → $50M cap + $10M gain cap + 5-yr 100%; POST-OBBBA (> 2025-07-04) → $75M cap + $15M gain cap + tiered 50%/75%/100%
Confirm corp gross assets were ≤ applicable cap IMMEDIATELY BEFORE AND AFTER stock issuance
Confirm stock acquired by original issuance (NOT secondary purchase)
Confirm holding period: pre-OBBBA needs > 5 yrs; post-OBBBA tiered — earliest 50% exclusion at 3 yrs
Confirm corp's trade/business is QUALIFIED (excludes professional services, hotels, restaurants, farming, mining, banking)
Date acquired: 100% exclusion for post-2010-09-27 acquisitions; 75% for 2010-02-18 to 2010-09-27; 50% for pre-2010-02-18
For post-OBBBA stock at 50%/75% tier: includible gain at 28% rate vs standard LTCG 15-20%
strategies.action: If client holds C-corp stock that meets §1202 qualified-small-business criteria, plan holding strategy per applicable rules (PRE-OBBBA = 5-yr binary 100%; POST-OBBBA = tiered 3/4/5-yr at 50/75/100%) to exclude up to applicable cap. Estimated heuristic savings on $1M assumed qualifying gain ~{{estSavings}} (LTCG + NIIT avoided).

strategies.name: §121 Primary residence sale exclusion ($250k/$500k)
strategies.trigger: H5 primary_residence asset with embedded gain (balance − costBasis) > $100,000 AND no existing home_sale_gross_gain_primary_residence adjustment (signal client isn't already selling this year).
strategies.formula: Exclude up to $250,000 single / $500,000 MFJ on gain from sale of primary residence (must have owned + lived there 2 of last 5 years). estSavings = min(embeddedGain, exclusionCap) × 0.20 (LTCG rate avoided). Plus 3.8% NIIT on the avoided portion.
prerequisiteData: Cost basis of home (purchase price + capital improvements + selling costs)
Date moved in + date plans to sell (2-of-last-5-years use-test)
Whether client has used §121 in the prior 2 years (only one §121 exclusion every 2 years)
Whether the home was ever a rental (depreciation recapture under §1250 NOT excluded by §121)
Whether nonqualified-use period applies (post-2008 rental periods reduce exclusion ratio)
strategies.action: Client has ~{{embeddedGain}} of embedded gain on primary residence. On future sale, §121 excludes up to {{exclusionCap}} — estimated tax saved ~{{estSavings}}. Plan to satisfy 2-of-last-5-years use test before sale.

strategies.name: §1031 like-kind exchange — defer cap gains on real estate
strategies.trigger: Schedule E rental gross income > $100k AND total income > $200k AND no existing section_1031_realized_gain adjustment
strategies.formula: Defer ordinary gain via like-kind exchange: identify replacement within 45 days, close within 180 days. estSavings = (assumed avg replacement gain $250k) × LTCG rate (~20%) + 3.8% NIIT × proxy 0.5 (timing value of deferral).
prerequisiteData: Specific property being sold + estimated FMV + adjusted basis
Qualified Intermediary engagement (cannot touch the sale proceeds)
Identification of up to 3 replacement properties within 45 days of closing
Funding to complete the replacement closing within 180 days
strategies.action: If client has a planned real estate sale, structure as a §1031 like-kind exchange via a Qualified Intermediary. Identify replacement within 45 days, close within 180 days. Estimated tax deferral on a $250k gain ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Pre-RMD Roth conversion ladder (age 60-72)
strategies.trigger: taxpayerAge between 60 and 72 AND H5 traditional/SEP/SIMPLE IRA total balance > $500k AND federal marginal rate < 32%
strategies.formula: Convert ~bracket headroom each year for years 60-72; reduces RMD base + future taxable distributions at potentially higher rates. estSavings = avg annual conversion × spread between current and projected RMD-age marginal rate × number of conversion years.
prerequisiteData: Confirm projected income trajectory through age 73 (SS, pensions, RMDs)
Estimated future marginal rate at RMD age (Pub 590-B life-expectancy table drives RMD %)
Cash flow for the conversion tax (paid from non-IRA assets to maximize Roth growth)
State residency plans through age 73 (state-tax arbitrage possible)
strategies.action: Execute an N-year Roth conversion ladder: convert ~{{annualConversion}}/year until age 73 to keep marginal rate ≤ {{topRate}}. Reduces RMD base by ~{{totalConversion}}; projected lifetime tax savings ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Cost segregation study — accelerated depreciation on real estate
strategies.irsPub: Pub 946; IRS Cost Segregation Audit Techniques Guide
strategies.trigger: Schedule E rental gross income > $100k AND no prior cost-segregation signal AND federal marginal rate ≥ 24%
strategies.formula: Segregate 5/7/15-year personal property + land improvements from 27.5/39-year building basis. Accelerates ~20-30% of basis. With TY2025 60% bonus depreciation: estSavings ≈ rental_gross × 0.25 × 0.60 × marginal_rate (one-time, but recurring as new properties acquired).
prerequisiteData: Most recent purchase price + closing date of each property
Engineering-based cost segregation study (qualified provider — Marshall & Stevens / KBKG / etc.)
Form 3115 (Change in Accounting Method) if applying to prior-year property — §481(a) catch-up adjustment
Confirm property is non-residential (39yr) or residential rental (27.5yr) and basis breakdown
strategies.action: Engage a qualified cost-seg engineer for a study on each major property. Reclassify ~25% of basis to 5/7/15-year buckets eligible for accelerated + bonus depreciation. TY{{taxYear}} bonus rate is {{bonusPct}}%. Estimated first-year benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Qualified Opportunity Zone investment — defer + reduce cap gains
strategies.trigger: Capital gains (LTCG + STCG) > $100,000 AND post-TCJA (TY2024+) AND no existing QOZ designation
strategies.formula: Invest realized capital gain in a Qualified Opportunity Fund within 180 days. Deferred until earlier of sale or 2026-12-31 for investments made on/before 2026-12-31; OBBBA OZ 2.0 gives post-2026 investments a rolling 5-year deferral + 10% basis step-up (30% rural). After 10 years held, the QOF investment's own appreciation is permanently tax-free. estSavings = deferred_gain × (LTCG rate + NIIT) × time-value-of-deferral proxy 0.3 + (long-term appreciation × LTCG × 1.0 if held 10+ years).
prerequisiteData: Date and amount of the eligible gain — 180-day clock starts at the sale date
QOF selection (publicly available registry; private QOF or self-organized)
Confirm QOF invests ≥ 90% of assets in a Qualified Opportunity Zone (audited semi-annually)
10-year holding plan — premature sale loses the basis step-up

OBBBA made the OZ program PERMANENT (OZ 2.0): gains invested on/before 2026-12-31 are still recognized 2026-12-31 (original statutory date); investments AFTER 2026-12-31 get a ROLLING 5-year deferral + 10% basis step-up (30% rural) under the new decennial zone designations (first effective 1/1/2027)
strategies.action: Within 180 days of a capital gain, roll {{deferredGain}} into a Qualified Opportunity Fund. Defers ~{{estSavings}} of current tax + locks in 10-year tax-free appreciation. Confirm QOF compliance + plan the 10-year hold.

strategies.name: Backdoor Roth IRA — for income above direct-Roth phase-out
strategies.trigger: AGI > Roth IRA contribution phase-out top (TY2024 $161k single / $240k MFJ; TY2025 $165k single / $246k MFJ) AND no existing nondeductible IRA contribution AND no/low pre-tax IRA balance (avoids pro-rata trap)
strategies.formula: Annual loop: contribute $7k (50+: $8k) nondeductible to traditional IRA → immediately convert to Roth (Form 8606 required). With ZERO pre-tax IRA balance: 100% tax-free. With pre-tax balance: pro-rata trap (see H6 Form 8606). TY2024 + TY2025: $7k base / $8k age 50+ (unchanged). Phase-out tops: TY2024 $161k single / $240k MFJ; TY2025 $165k single / $246k MFJ. estSavings = $7k × growth × future_rate (long-term).
prerequisiteData: Confirm $0 balance in all pre-tax IRAs (traditional / SEP / SIMPLE) at year-end — otherwise §408(d)(2) pro-rata applies and most of the conversion is taxable
Form 8606 filing for both the nondeductible contribution AND the conversion (Part I + Part II)
If client has pre-tax IRA balance, consider rolling it to 401(k) first (employer plans escape §408(d)(2) aggregation)
Contribution deadline: April 15 of next year for the prior tax year
strategies.action: Contribute {{contributionAmount}} nondeductible to a Traditional IRA, then immediately convert to Roth (Form 8606). If pre-tax IRA balance > $0, address pro-rata trap first (roll to 401(k) or accept tax cost). Long-term Roth-growth benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Conservation easement (with risk warning)
strategies.trigger: Owns real estate with conservation potential (rural / scenic / historic) — heuristic informational only.
strategies.formula: Donate a perpetual easement on real property → deduction = decline in FMV (full FMV - encumbered FMV). estSavings = deduction × marginal rate, but capped at 50% AGI (general property rule, with 15-year carryforward).
prerequisiteData: **HIGH IRS AUDIT RISK** — syndicated conservation easements are LISTED TRANSACTIONS under IRS Notice 2017-10. Strong scrutiny + penalties.
Qualified appraisal (must satisfy §170(h)(1)(A) qualified-organization recipient)
Perpetuity requirement: easement runs with the land forever
Boundary survey + baseline documentation report
Land trust acceptance + monitoring agreement
Client risk tolerance — IRS may challenge valuation
strategies.action: **HIGH AUDIT-RISK STRATEGY.** Only pursue if client owns suitable land + has high charitable intent + is prepared for IRS scrutiny. Engage qualified appraiser (§170(h) compliant) + reputable land trust. Document baseline conservation values. Consider a 4:1 maximum deduction ratio for syndicated deals per Section 605 limits in SECURE 2.0.

strategies.name: Maximize HSA contribution (triple-tax benefit)
strategies.irsPub: Pub 969; Rev. Proc. 2023-23 (TY2024 limits $4,150 self / $8,300 family); Rev. Proc. 2024-25 (TY2025 limits $4,300 self / $8,550 family); Rev. Proc. 2025-19 (TY2026 limits $4,400 self / $8,750 family); $1,000 age-55+ catch-up all years
strategies.trigger: hsaIsFamilyCoverage flag set (proxy for HDHP) AND current hsa_contribution < §223 cap (incl age-55+ catch-up)
strategies.formula: Triple-tax-advantaged: (1) above-the-line deduction now, (2) tax-free growth, (3) tax-free qualified medical withdrawals. TY2024 caps: $4,150 self / $8,300 family. TY2025 caps: $4,300 self / $8,550 family. TY2026 caps: $4,400 self / $8,750 family. $1,000 age-55+ catch-up. estSavings = (cap - current) × (federal + state marginal rate).
prerequisiteData: Confirm HDHP enrollment for the full year (or pro-rate per last-month rule)
Existing employer HSA contribution (W-2 Box 12 code W) — counts against the cap
Whether spouse is also HSA-eligible (separate caps if both have HDHP)
strategies.action: Max the HSA: contribute the remaining ~{{contributionGap}} by April 15 of next year to reach the {{cap}} cap. Estimated savings ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: In-plan Roth conversion — convert 401(k) pre-tax to Roth within same plan
strategies.trigger: H5 401k_traditional balance > $25,000 AND age between 30 and 72 AND federalMarginalRate <= 0.22 (current bracket favorable) AND NOT MFS.
strategies.formula: In-plan transfer of pre-tax 401(k) balance to designated Roth 401(k) account in SAME plan. Taxable at current marginal rate in conversion year. Distinct from G1.4 (IRA-level) and G1.26 (backdoor IRA). Heuristic conversion = $25,000 (one-bracket increment); cost = $25,000 × currentMarginalRate. Long-term benefit (tax-free growth + tax-free qualified distributions) deferred to retirement.
prerequisiteData: Confirm 401(k) plan offers designated Roth account (most modern plans do — required for in-plan conversion)
