Confirm 401(k) plan permits in-plan conversion (some plans require separation from service; Notice 2010-84 expanded to allow in-service for pre-tax sub-account)
Current marginal rate vs projected retirement rate (must converge favorably for net benefit)
Available cash OUTSIDE the 401(k) to pay the conversion tax (CRITICAL — paying tax with withholding from 401(k) defeats the purpose; reduces converted amount)
Coordinate with G1.16 mega-backdoor Roth (in-plan conversion of after-tax sub-account) and G1.4 IRA Roth conversion
5-year clock per §402A(c)(4)(F): each conversion starts its own 5-year clock for tax-free qualified distribution
No reversal: pre-TCJA, in-plan conversions could be recharacterized; TCJA repealed recharacterization (TY2018+) — election is IRREVOCABLE
strategies.action: Elect in-plan Roth conversion of ~{{conversionAmount}} from pre-tax 401(k) to designated Roth 401(k) sub-account this year. Current-year tax cost ~{{costThisYear}} at {{currentRate}}% marginal; long-term tax-free Roth growth + qualified distribution in retirement. Pay conversion tax with OUTSIDE funds.

strategies.name: §174A R&D expensing (OBBBA 2025) + foreign R&D 15-yr amortization + transition relief
strategies.irsPub: OBBBA §70302 (signed 2025-07-04); Rev. Proc. 2025-28; Notice 2023-63; Notice 2024-12; Form 4562; Form 6765
strategies.trigger: netSeIncome > $200,000 AND adjustments include qbi_income (proxy for active trade or business) AND no existing §41 R&D credit (G1.36).
strategies.formula: POST-OBBBA (signed 2025-07-04): §174A REPEALED mandatory capitalization of DOMESTIC R&D for TY2025+ — full §162 expensing restored. Election to amortize over 5 yrs available. FOREIGN R&D still 15-yr amortization under §174. TRANSITION RELIEF: taxpayers can deduct unamortized 2022-2024 DOMESTIC R&E expenditures over remaining amortization period OR elect to deduct ratably over 1-yr (small biz < $31M gross receipts) or 2-yr period per Rev. Proc. 2025-28. Strategy: (a) for TY2025+ domestic, expense fully under §174A; (b) for 2022-2024 unamortized basis, elect transition deduction; (c) Form 6765 §41 R&D credit + §280C(c) reduced-credit election; (d) §59(e)(2) optional 10-yr election still relevant for FOREIGN R&D. Heuristic estSavings = $80,000 typical R&D bucket × marginal rate (full expensing TY2025+).
prerequisiteData: Detailed breakdown of R&D-tagged expenses: pre-development design + experimentation (true §174) vs post-development support + marketing + sales (§162)
Foreign R&D portion (15-yr amortization vs 5-yr domestic — Reg §1.174-2)
Accounting method election (Form 3115 for change in accounting method if reclassifying — §481(a) adjustment)
Form 6765 §41 R&D credit calc — 20% incremental OR 14% Alternative Simplified Credit (ASC); 4-part test (qualified research per §41(d))
§59(e)(2) optional 10-yr election — spreads §174 amortization to bypass AMT depreciation preference (line 2i Form 6251)
Coordinate with G1.36 (R&D credit) + G1.60 (§41(h) payroll-tax election for small biz)
Documentation burden: §174 audit risk requires contemporaneous research log + qualified research expense detail
strategies.action: Reclassify ~30% of R&D-tagged expenses to §162 ordinary (post-development support, marketing, sales) via Form 3115 change in accounting method. Combine with §41 credit (Form 6765) + §59(e) optional 10-yr election. Estimated current-year benefit ~{{estSavings}} from accelerated deduction + R&D credit.

strategies.name: Year-end income deferral / acceleration — bracket timing
strategies.trigger: adjustedGrossIncome > $50,000 AND federalMarginalRate >= 0.22 AND taxableIncome within $20,000 of a federal bracket boundary (10/12/22/24/32/35/37%).
strategies.formula: Cash-method taxpayer can shift discretionary income/deductions across Dec 31 to optimize bracket. Heuristic: $10,000 typical shift × bracket spread (12%-22% = 10%; 22%-24% = 2%; 32%-35% = 3%; 35%-37% = 2%). Strategy depends on current vs next-year marginal — if next year higher, accelerate income + defer deductions; if next year lower, defer income + accelerate deductions.
prerequisiteData: Projected next-year income trajectory (rising, falling, same)
Discretionary income items: SE invoicing timing (cash-method client), bonus deferral availability (W-2 employer-dependent), gain realization (sell now vs January)
Discretionary deductions: prepay state income tax (capped at SALT $10k), property tax (SALT $10k cap), charitable December gifts (G1.3 bunching coordinates)
§461(g) restriction — prepaid expenses limited to 12-month rule
Constructive receipt doctrine (Reg §1.451-2) — cannot defer income already 'constructively received' (e.g. December bonus check sitting on desk)
AMT considerations — accelerating state tax doesn't help if AMT applies (state tax is AMT preference)
Coordinate with G1.4 Roth (bracket-fill opposite direction), G1.3 bunching (deduction acceleration), G1.6 NIIT cliff (income deferral past $200k/$250k)
strategies.action: Project {{projectedNextYear}} taxable income vs current {{currentTaxable}}. {{strategyDirection}} ~$10k of discretionary income/deductions across Dec 31 to optimize bracket arbitrage. Estimated annual benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Bargain sale to charity §1011(b) — partial-sale / partial-donation
strategies.trigger: longTermGains > $50,000 OR H5 primary_residence OR real_estate H5 asset with embedded gain > $100,000 AND assumed charitable intent.
strategies.formula: Sell appreciated property to qualified §501(c)(3) charity for LESS than FMV. Per §1011(b): basis allocated proportionally between sale (sale_price / FMV portion) and gift (1 - sale_price/FMV portion). Gain = sale_price − (basis × sale_price/FMV). Charitable deduction = FMV − sale_price (capped at 30% AGI for LTCG property to public charity). Heuristic: $200k FMV asset / $50k basis / $50k sale price → gain $50k − ($50k × $50k/$200k) = $50k − $12,500 = $37,500 gain @ LTCG vs $150k charity deduction.
prerequisiteData: Qualified appraisal per §170(f)(11)(C) — required for ALL non-cash gifts > $5,000 (Form 8283 Sec B for > $5,000; appraiser signs Part III for > $5,000)
Property type (real estate, art, securities, intellectual property — each has §170 valuation rules)
Holding period > 1 yr (LTCG-favored) — STCG bargain sale loses §170(e)(1) FMV deduction (only basis deductible)
Charity acceptance — charity must accept the property + perform §170(e)(1)(B)(i) 3-yr holding (sales-deduction recapture for tangible personal property if charity disposes < 3 yrs)
AGI limits: 30% for LTCG property to public charity (50% to private foundation); 5-yr carryforward per §170(d)(1) if exceeds limit
Form 8283 Section B Part I + appraisal attachment for >$5k; charity signs Part IV acknowledgment
State conformity to §1011(b) — most states follow federal; check state-specific bargain-sale treatment
AMT preference — itemized deductions for charitable are allowed for AMT (NOT a preference), but cap gain on sale portion adds to AMTI
strategies.action: Sell {{assetDescription}} (FMV ~{{fmv}}, basis ~{{basis}}) to {{charityName}} for ${{salePrice}}. Per §1011(b): {{salePortion}}% sale → ${{recognizedGain}} LTCG; {{donationPortion}}% donation → ${{charitableDeduction}} deduction. Net combined benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: ISO lot selection — qualifying disposition (>2 yr grant + >1 yr exercise)
strategies.irsPub: Pub 525 (Taxable + Nontaxable Income); Form 3921 (ISO exercise statement)
strategies.trigger: H5 iso_amt_credit_shares balance > $25,000 OR adjustments include amt_iso_bargain_element > 0 (ISO exercise occurred).
strategies.formula: ISO §422(a) qualifying disposition requires hold BOTH: (a) > 2 yrs from grant date AND (b) > 1 yr from exercise date. Sale satisfying BOTH → entire gain (sale − strike) at LTCG. Sale missing either → §421(b) disqualifying disposition: ordinary income on lesser of (FMV exercise − strike) or (sale − strike); excess at capital gain. Strategy: track per-lot grant + exercise dates; ONLY sell qualified-eligible lots first (FIFO of qualified, not FIFO of all). Distinct from G1.5 (AMT at exercise) — this is at SALE. Heuristic: $50k spread × (24% ord − 15% LTCG) = $4,500 per $50k qualifying-vs-disqualifying.
prerequisiteData: Per-lot grant dates (Form 3921 box 1) — qualified disposition requires > 2 yrs from grant
Per-lot exercise dates (Form 3921 box 2) — qualified disposition requires > 1 yr from exercise
Per-lot FMV at exercise (Form 3921 box 4) vs strike (box 3) — determines bargain element + disqualifying ordinary income amount
Specific-share-identification election per Reg §1.1012-1(c) — broker must accept SSI before sale (default is FIFO without election)
Coordinate with G1.5 AMT-ISO timing (at EXERCISE) vs G1.71 (at SALE) — G1.5 handles exercise-year AMT; G1.71 handles disposition-year character
Coordinate with G1.56 Specific-Share-ID (broader lot-rotation framework)
$100k §422(d) annual exercise limit — exercises > $100k FMV/yr lose ISO status (NSO treatment instead — already ordinary income)
AMT credit (Form 8801) — bargain element creates AMT in exercise year; recoverable via §53 credit when ordinary tax exceeds AMT in future years (E2 Phase E)
strategies.action: Track per-lot ISO grant + exercise dates. For ~{{eligibleLots}} qualifying-eligible lots (> 2 yrs grant + > 1 yr exercise), elect specific-share-identification with broker BEFORE sale and sell those first. Estimated annual benefit ~{{estSavings}} (LTCG vs ordinary rate spread).

strategies.name: RSU sell-to-cover withholding gap — Q4 estimated tax adjustment
strategies.trigger: W-2 wages > $300,000 (tech-salary proxy for embedded RSU vesting) AND federalMarginalRate >= 0.32.
strategies.formula: RSU vest = §83(a) FMV income (no §83(b) election available post-vest). Employer withholds at §3402(g)(1)(A) flat 22% supplemental rate (37% if > $1M YTD). For high-income employees in 32-37% brackets, the 22% rate UNDER-withholds by 10-15%. Withholding gap = RSU income × (marginal rate − 22%). Pay Q4 estimated tax to avoid §6654 underpayment penalty (or rely on 110% prior-year safe harbor — G1.52). Heuristic gap = $200,000 typical RSU × 10% marginal-vs-withholding spread = $20,000 gap.
prerequisiteData: Actual RSU income amount (W-2 Box 1 includes; Box 12 code V reports RSU only)
Year-to-date wages — at $1M YTD, supplemental rate jumps to 37% per §3402(g)(1)(B)
Federal marginal rate at full taxable income (after RSU + other comp + investment income)
Prior-year tax liability — §6654 safe harbor (100% prior / 110% if AGI > $150k)
Q4 estimated tax payment timing (Jan 15 due; pay by Dec 31 if state-tax acceleration helps)
Coordinate with G1.52 estimated-tax safe-harbor + G1.6 NIIT cliff (RSU vest may push past $200k/$250k threshold)
Sell-to-cover ratio — broker auto-sells enough shares to cover the 22% withholding, leaving remainder
strategies.action: RSU vest of ~{{rsuIncome}} withheld at 22% supplemental rate but client's marginal rate is {{marginalRate}}%. Estimated under-withholding gap ~{{estSavings}}. Pay Q4 estimated tax by Jan 15 to avoid §6654 penalty.

strategies.name: NUA in-service distribution (age 55-59½) — separated from service
strategies.irsPub: Pub 575 (Pension and Annuity Income); Rev. Rul. 74-205
strategies.trigger: H5 employer_stock balance > $50,000 AND taxpayerAge BETWEEN 55 AND 59 AND distinct from G1.15 (which is at/after 59½).
strategies.formula: §402(e)(4) NUA election — lump-sum distribution of employer stock from 401(k) at separation. Cost basis taxed at ordinary rate in distribution year; NUA appreciation deferred + taxed at LTCG when stock sold later. §72(t)(2)(A)(v) separation-from-service exception waives 10% early-withdrawal penalty for age 55+ (technically 'rule of 55'). NUA savings = NUA appreciation × (marginal − 15% LTCG). Heuristic $200k NUA × (24% − 15%) = $18,000.
prerequisiteData: Cost basis of employer stock within 401(k) (employer-provided statement)
Current FMV of employer stock (broker statement at distribution)
NUA = FMV − basis (the appreciation portion eligible for LTCG)
MUST be lump-sum distribution per §402(e)(4)(D) — partial distributions disqualify
MUST distribute ALL plan assets in same year — including non-stock; can roll non-stock to IRA per Rev. Rul. 96-49
Triggering event: separation from service, age 59½, death, disability (one of these MUST occur in year)
Distinguish from G1.15 (NUA at full retirement) — G1.73 is the in-service window age 55-59½
§72(t)(2)(A)(v) 'rule of 55' — separation from service at age 55+ avoids 10% early-withdrawal penalty
Coordinate with G1.4 Roth (lump-sum spreading), G1.22 pre-RMD ladder (early-retirement planning)
Estate planning — NUA stock can be held until death; basis stepped up to FMV (NUA appreciation never taxed)
strategies.action: Elect NUA on {{nuaAmount}} of employer stock at separation. Basis ~{{basisAmount}} taxed at ordinary; appreciation ~{{nuaAppreciation}} deferred + at LTCG. Estimated benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: §45S Paid Family Medical Leave Credit — PERMANENT post-OBBBA + 2026 changes
strategies.trigger: netSeIncome > $250,000 (proxy: business large enough to have employees on payroll) AND no existing §45S adjustment.
strategies.formula: §45S credit MADE PERMANENT by OBBBA (signed 2025-07-04). Credit = 12.5% × wages paid during qualifying family + medical leave, scaling UP to 25% if leave wages = 100% of normal. Up to 12 weeks of paid leave per employee per year. Qualifying employee = > 1 yr employed + 60% of HCE threshold prior-yr wages ($96,000 TY2025 / $99,000 TY2026 — OBBBA tightened from $84k TY2024). TY2026+: NEW employer election between (a) Wages Paid method or (b) Insurance Premium Method (claims credit based on insurance premiums covering FMLA). Heuristic: 5 employees × 2 weeks paid leave × $1,000/wk × 25% = $2,500 credit.
prerequisiteData: Written §45S policy — must exist in writing BEFORE leave taken
Policy must cover all qualifying employees (no discrimination)
Minimum 2 weeks of paid leave per year
Paid leave wages ≥ 50% of employee's normal rate
Qualifying employee: employed > 1 yr + prior-yr compensation < $84,000 TY2024 (Rev. Proc. 2023-34)
Leave reasons per FMLA §102(a) — birth, adoption, family illness, military deployment, etc.
Credit rate scales 12.5%-25% based on leave-wage % of normal rate (formula in §45S(a)(1)(A))
Cap: 12 weeks of leave per employee per year
Coordinate with §3306(c)(20) FUTA exemption for family leave wages
Form 8994 + carryforward via §38 general business credit pool
strategies.action: If you have employees on payroll, adopt written §45S paid family leave policy (≥2 wk paid leave at ≥50% normal rate). Heuristic 5 employees × 2 wk × $1k × 25% = ~{{estSavings}} credit on Form 8994.

strategies.name: WOTC §51 Work Opportunity Tax Credit — hire from targeted groups
strategies.trigger: netSeIncome > $250,000 (proxy: hires likely) AND no existing WOTC adjustment.
strategies.formula: §51 credit = 25%-40% × first-year wages for qualifying hires from targeted groups (food stamp, long-term unemployed, vocational rehab, ex-felons, summer youth, ssi recipients, TANF recipients, qualified veterans). Standard credit: 40% × first $6,000 wages = $2,400 per non-vet hire (if work 400+ hrs in first year). Qualified veteran tiers: up to $9,600 for service-disabled vet with 6+ mo unemployment (50% × $24k wages). Form 8850 pre-screening MUST be done within 28 days of hire.
prerequisiteData: Form 8850 IRS pre-screening form — completed BY EMPLOYEE on or BEFORE day of job offer (28-day window after start to submit to state DOL)
ETA Form 9061 (Individual Characteristics Form) — submitted with 8850 to state workforce agency
State workforce agency CERTIFIES eligibility — typically 4-12 weeks turnaround

Targeted groups (§51(d)): IV-A recipient (TANF), qualified veteran, ex-felon, designated community resident, vocational rehab referral, summer youth, SNAP recipient, SSI recipient, long-term family assistance recipient, qualified long-term unemployment recipient
Wage formula: 25% × first $6k wages (work 120-400 hrs) OR 40% × first $6k (work 400+ hrs). Qualified vet variants per §51(d)(3).
Form 5884 + carryforward via §38 general business credit pool (20-yr carryforward + 1-yr carryback)
§280C(a) — must reduce wage deduction by credit amount (no double-dipping)
WOTC currently authorized through 2025-12-31 per §51(c)(4) — re-extension possible
strategies.action: Screen new hires against §51 targeted groups via Form 8850 (within 28 days of hire date). Heuristic 2 qualifying hires × $2,400 credit each = ~{{estSavings}} on Form 5884.

strategies.name: §170(h) qualified conservation easement — non-syndicated genuine landowner
strategies.trigger: H5 real_estate balance > $500,000 (large land holding — genuine landowner) AND NO syndicated-easement marker (distinct from G1.20).
strategies.formula: §170(h) qualified conservation contribution: perpetual easement on qualified real property to qualified organization for one of four §170(h)(4) purposes (outdoor recreation, scenic enjoyment, historic preservation, open-space). Deduction = FMV of donated easement (FMV-with vs FMV-without). 50% AGI cap per §170(b)(1)(E)(i); 100% for QUALIFIED RANCHERS / FARMERS per §170(b)(1)(E)(iv); 15-yr carryforward per §170(b)(1)(E)(ii). DISTINCT from G1.20 syndicated easement (Notice 2017-10 listed transaction). Genuine landowner = low audit risk if appraisal sound. Heuristic: $1M easement × 35% marginal = $350,000 deduction-driven savings (spread over multi-year cap).
prerequisiteData: Qualified APPRAISAL per §170(f)(11)(C) — required for ALL non-cash > $5,000. For easements > $5,000, appraiser signs Form 8283 Sec B Part III + IRS receives appraisal copy.
Qualified organization per §170(h)(3): government unit, public charity, or §170(h)(3)(B) qualified land trust
Conservation purpose per §170(h)(4)(A): outdoor recreation OR scenic enjoyment OR historic preservation OR open-space (protection of farmland / habitat)
Perpetuity test per §170(h)(5)(A): easement must run with land in perpetuity (no time limit)
Mortgage subordination — lender must subordinate any mortgage to easement

AGI cap: 50% per §170(b)(1)(E)(i) for individuals (vs 30% other LTCG property). 100% for QUALIFIED RANCHERS / FARMERS per §170(b)(1)(E)(iv) — defined as gross income from ranching/farming > 50% of total.
15-yr carryforward per §170(b)(1)(E)(ii) (vs 5-yr standard §170(d)(1))
Form 8283 Sec B + Form 8283-V supplemental + appraisal narrative + baseline documentation + easement deed
AUDIT RISK: distinct from SYNDICATED (Notice 2017-10) — genuine landowner low risk IF appraisal supported by comparable sales + best-use analysis
State conformity varies — most states follow federal §170 limits
strategies.action: Donate qualified perpetual conservation easement on {{landAcres}} acres to qualified land trust. FMV deduction ~{{easementValue}} subject to 50% AGI cap (100% if rancher/farmer per §170(b)(1)(E)(iv)). Estimated multi-year savings ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Self-rental grouping §1.469-4(d) — convert passive rental to non-passive
strategies.trigger: K-1 active trade/business income > $50,000 AND scheduleEPassiveLossSuspended > $10,000 (rental losses suspended) AND no existing G1.18 REPS flag.
strategies.formula: §1.469-4(d)(1) grouping election: combine rental activity with trade/business in which taxpayer materially participates → ENTIRE grouping treated as non-passive per §469(c)(7). Suspended rental PAL release immediately deductible against active income. Heuristic: $20,000 typical suspended PAL × marginal = $4,800 (at 24%). Distinct from G1.18 REPS election (which requires 750 hrs + > 50% time).
prerequisiteData: Material participation in the trade/business (one of 7 tests per Reg §1.469-5T)
Rental property rented to the same trade/business (or related party) — self-rental rule §1.469-2(f)(6)
§1.469-4(c) appropriate economic unit test — must show 'organizational/economic unity' (5 factors)
Disclosure statement attached to return per Rev. Proc. 2010-13 (Notice 2008-64) — election irrevocable
§1.469-4(d)(1) grouping CANNOT undo without 'compelling circumstances' OR IRS consent
Distinct from G1.18 REPS election (750 hrs + > 50% personal services test)
Self-rental rule §1.469-2(f)(6) — net positive self-rental income is non-passive (avoids §469 limit) but net negative loss STAYS passive without grouping
Coordinate with §199A — self-rental + active business can be aggregated for §199A wage/UBIA limits per Reg §1.199A-4
strategies.action: Make Reg §1.469-4(d)(1) grouping election attaching disclosure statement: combine rental + trade/business as ONE economic unit. Releases ~{{suspendedPal}} of suspended PAL against active income. Estimated savings ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Multi-state nonresident income allocation — sourcing optimization
strategies.irsPub: State-specific nonresident filing instructions (CA 540NR; NY IT-203; MA Form 1-NR/PY)
strategies.trigger: totalIncome > $200,000 AND has W-2 in different state than resident state (stateCode != client.state) OR remote-worker scenario.
strategies.formula: Multi-state filers with income earned in multiple jurisdictions can optimize sourcing per state's apportionment rules. Strategies: (a) verify proper allocation (wages where SERVICE PERFORMED, not where paid); (b) use credit-for-tax-paid-to-other-state per §164(a)(3) state codes; (c) document remote-work days for convenience-of-employer rule states (NY, NJ, DE, NE, PA — 'convenience' = sourced to employer state even if worked elsewhere). Heuristic: $200k W-2 × 5% over-sourcing × 9% state marginal = ~$900/yr. Real range: $0 to $50k+ for high-income remote workers.
prerequisiteData: Work-location calendar — days physically worked in each state (PRECISION CRITICAL for NY/NJ allocations)
Convenience-of-employer rule states: NY, NJ, DE, NE, PA (CT for COVID-period; AR partial). Days 'voluntarily' worked outside the employer state may STILL be sourced there.
State allocation methods: NY uses 'business carried on' days; CA uses 'time of personal services performed'; NJ 'employee's days worked here'
Reciprocity agreements (skip nonresident filing): IL/IN, KY/IL/IN/MI/OH/VA/WV, MD/PA/VA/WV/DC, NJ/PA (limited), VA/DC/PA/WV (commuter), MI/IL/IN/MN/OH/WI, ND/MN/MT
Resident-state credit-for-tax-paid (§164(a)(3) federal pickup; state-specific credit-for-tax-paid-to-other-state — usually capped at TAX-WHERE-EARNED computed at resident-state rate)
K-1 sourcing — apportion partnership income by partnership's apportionment factor (sales / payroll / property — UDITPA 3-factor or single-sales-factor)
Telecommuter agreements — some states (NM, OR) signed MTC compact allowing in-state work days NOT to source
Coordinate with G1.58 state residency change + part-year residency split (Phase E E12)
strategies.action: For multi-state W-2 ({{primaryState}} resident + {{secondaryState}} work), verify allocation per work-days calendar + state convenience rule. Estimated annual benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: §453 partial-installment election — opt out of installment for higher-bracket years
strategies.trigger: Existing G1.47 installment sale (real_estate gain > $250k AND AGI > $250k) AND federalMarginalRate >= 0.32.
strategies.formula: §453(d) ELECTION OUT — installment sale taxpayer can elect OUT of automatic §453 deferral and recognize ENTIRE gain in year of sale. Useful when: (a) current-year bracket lower than future bracket (front-load gain); (b) net operating loss carryforward absorbs gain; (c) §1031 partial deferral already chosen. Distinct from G1.47 (which uses automatic §453 installment). Heuristic: $500k typical gain × (24% future − 22% current) = $10,000.
prerequisiteData: Sale qualifies for installment treatment per §453(a) — disposition of property where ANY payment is received in tax year AFTER year of sale
Gross-profit ratio per §453(c) = (selling price - basis - selling expenses) / (selling price - mortgages assumed by buyer)
Election OUT per §453(d) — irrevocable, must be made by due date (with extensions) of return for year of sale
Recapture per §453(i) — depreciation recapture (§1245 / §1250 / §291) is FULLY recognized in year of sale REGARDLESS of installment election
§453A interest charge — applies to installment sales > $5M individual / $10M corp ('large installment sales'); deferred installment tax accrues interest at AFR
Related-party installment sale per §453(g) — buyer can't resell within 2 yrs (§453(e) acceleration)
Coordinate with G1.47 (default installment) + G4.4 capital-loss carryforward (offset acceleration)
Distinct from G1.21 §1031 (which defers gain via exchange) + G1.45 §121 home sale (which excludes gain)
strategies.action: Existing installment sale with current marginal {{currentRate}}% vs projected next-year {{projectedRate}}%. Elect OUT under §453(d) to recognize ENTIRE gain ~{{totalGain}} in lower-bracket year. Estimated benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: §47 Historic Rehabilitation Credit — 20% credit on certified rehab
strategies.irsPub: Form 3468 (Investment Credit); Pub 535; NPS Historic Tax Credit guidance
strategies.trigger: H5 real_estate with cost_basis > $250,000 OR netSeIncome > $100,000 AND tag for historic rehab.
strategies.formula: §47 credit = 20% × Qualified Rehabilitation Expenditures (QREs) on certified historic structure. TCJA changes: 20% credit spread over 5 yrs (4% per yr) starting in year placed in service per §47(a)(2). Pre-TCJA 10% non-historic credit REPEALED for property placed in service after 2017. Heuristic: $500k typical QRE × 20% = $100,000 credit spread over 5 yrs = $20,000/yr.
prerequisiteData: Structure must be CERTIFIED HISTORIC STRUCTURE per §47(c)(3) — listed on National Register OR contributing to registered historic district
Rehabilitation must be CERTIFIED REHABILITATION per §47(c)(2)(C) — Secretary of Interior approval via NPS Form 10-168 Parts 1/2/3
Qualified Rehabilitation Expenditures per §47(c)(2)(A) — depreciable structure (NOT building acquisition cost, NOT enlargement, NOT facade easement)
Substantial-rehabilitation test per §47(c)(1)(A) — QREs > greater of $5,000 OR adjusted basis, within 24-mo period (60-mo phased)
Owner must hold for 5 yrs after placed in service per §50(a)(1)(A) — disposal before 5 yrs triggers recapture
Pre-TCJA 10% non-historic credit REPEALED per TCJA §13402 (placed in service after 2017)
TCJA spread: 20% credit recognized 20% per year over 5 yrs (was lump-sum pre-TCJA)
Coordinate with §168 depreciation — credit basis reduction per §50(c)(1) reduces depreciable basis
State-specific historic credits stack (35 states have own program) — coordinate with state historic preservation office
strategies.action: If certified historic rehab (NPS Form 10-168 approval): 20% credit on QRE ~{{qre}} = ${{totalCredit}} spread over 5 yrs ({{annualCredit}}/yr). Estimated first-year benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: §44 Disabled Access Credit — small business ADA accommodations
strategies.trigger: netSeIncome > $50,000 AND netSeIncome < $1,000,000 (small biz threshold) AND no existing §44 credit.
strategies.formula: §44 credit = 50% × eligible access expenditures BETWEEN $250 and $10,250 = max $5,000/yr credit. Eligible small biz: prior-yr gross receipts < $1M OR < 30 full-time employees. Eligible expenses per §44(c)(2): ADA accommodations (remove barriers, provide interpreters, modify equipment, provide alternative formats). Heuristic: $5,250 typical accommodation × 50% over $250 = $2,500 credit.
prerequisiteData: Eligible small business per §44(b): prior-yr gross receipts ≤ $1,000,000 OR ≤ 30 full-time employees

Eligible access expenditures per §44(c)(2): (a) remove architectural / physical barriers; (b) provide qualified interpreters / readers for hearing/visual impaired; (c) modify equipment; (d) provide alternative formats; (e) other reasonable accommodations
Expense range: > $250 AND ≤ $10,250 per year (credit = 50% × (expense − $250))
Expenses MUST be paid/incurred to comply with ADA per 42 USC §12101+
§44(d)(7) — disallowed if eligible for §190 deduction (architectural barrier removal — alternate path)
Form 8826 + §38 general business credit (20-yr carryforward)
Disabled access credit + §190 deduction coordination — choose ONE: credit (50% × amount > $250) OR deduction (immediate $15k §190 expense)
Coordinate with state ADA compliance grants
strategies.action: Document {{accommodationDescription}} ADA expense {{expenseAmount}}. §44 credit = 50% × ({{expenseAmount}} − $250) = {{estSavings}} on Form 8826.

strategies.name: §1374 Built-In Gains tax — newly-elected S-corp 5-yr recognition period
strategies.trigger: K-1 from S-corp with entityType s_corp AND active K-1 income > $50,000 AND assumed recent C-to-S conversion (proxy — flag for CPA).
strategies.formula: §1374 BIG tax = 21% × MIN(net recognized BIG, taxable income computed as if C-corp). Applies to newly-elected S-corps for 5 yrs after election (recognition period). Net recognized BIG = (FMV of asset at conversion − basis) recognized at disposition. Strategy: HOLD appreciated assets past 5-yr recognition period (per §1374(d)(7)) OR offset with built-in LOSSES (§1374(d)(4)). Flows through K-1 to shareholder as reduction in pass-through income.
prerequisiteData: S-corp election date (Form 2553) — 5-yr recognition period runs from FIRST DAY of first S-corp year
FMV of EACH asset at conversion date (basis for built-in gain/loss tracking)
C-corp earnings & profits at conversion (could trigger §1375 passive investment income tax on top of §1374 BIG)
Asset-by-asset basis schedule maintained over recognition period
Built-in LOSSES per §1374(d)(4) — offsets net recognized BIG (limited to losses recognized within recognition period)
Recognition period: 5 yrs per §1374(d)(7) (was 10 yrs pre-2009; permanent reduction by PATH Act 2015)
Tax computed at entity level (21% C-corp rate per §1374(b)(1)) — flows through reducing K-1 income to shareholders
Coordinate with G1.17 (S-corp reasonable comp), G1.83 (§338(h)(10) which is OPPOSITE — asset sale election)
Form 1120-S Schedule D + Form 8869 (recognized BIG attached statement)
strategies.action: If your S-corp converted from C-corp within 5 yrs, hold appreciated assets PAST the recognition period to avoid 21% §1374 BIG tax. Estimated annual shareholder benefit ~{{estSavings}} per $100k of avoided recognition.

strategies.name: §338(h)(10) election — S-corp stock sale treated as asset sale
strategies.trigger: K-1 from S-corp with active K-1 income > $100,000 AND high LTCG > $250,000 (proxy for likely stock-sale year) AND netSeIncome < $100,000 (not the entity owner W-2'd themselves).
strategies.formula: §338(h)(10) joint election (buyer + ALL S-corp shareholders) — TREAT stock sale as deemed asset sale for tax purposes. Seller's gain: ordinary on §1245/§1250 recapture + cap gain on goodwill/§197 intangibles. BUYER GETS STEP-UP BASIS in assets (depreciable + amortizable). Bilateral negotiation: buyer typically pays seller more cash to compensate seller for character conversion (some ordinary income vs all LTCG without election). Heuristic: $1M deal × 30% recapture × (37% ord − 23.8% NIIT-included LTCG) = $39,600 character cost; buyer pays $50k+ premium.
prerequisiteData: Sale is QUALIFIED STOCK PURCHASE per §338(d)(3) — 80%+ stock purchase within 12-mo period
Joint election BY BOTH BUYER (corporation) AND ALL S-corp shareholders — Form 8023 within 8.5 mo of acquisition
Asset allocation under §1060 residual method — Form 8883 + appraisal
Character analysis: §1245 / §1250 / §291 depreciation recapture is ORDINARY; goodwill / §197 is CAP GAIN
Bilateral price negotiation — buyer typically pays MORE cash to compensate seller for character conversion
Seller-side state tax — many states track federal; CA / NY allow §338(h)(10) recognition deferred
Distinct from §338(g) (one-sided election, no asset-sale treatment for seller — generally unfavorable for individuals)
Coordinate with G1.82 §1374 BIG (which DEFERS recognition; §338(h)(10) ACCELERATES via asset-sale treatment)
Foreign buyers — §338(g) more common (no §338(h)(10) due to S-corp ownership restrictions)
strategies.action: If exiting S-corp via stock sale: negotiate §338(h)(10) joint election with buyer (Form 8023 within 8.5 mo). Estimated character cost vs buyer-paid premium ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: §351 controlled-corp contribution — tax-free property transfer to corporation
strategies.trigger: H5 real_estate balance > $500,000 OR appreciated H5 brokerage_taxable > $250,000 AND no existing entity formation marker.
strategies.formula: §351 tax-free contribution: transferor(s) of property to controlled corporation (≥ 80% voting + ≥ 80% other classes per §368(c)) defer gain IF the transfer is solely for stock (§351(b) boot triggers recognition to extent of boot). Transferor basis in stock = transferred property basis (§358). Corporation basis in property = transferor basis + gain recognized (§362). Heuristic: $500k FMV / $100k basis × 23.8% LTCG-NIIT = $95,200 deferred (PV).
prerequisiteData: Transferor (or group) must control ≥ 80% voting + ≥ 80% other classes per §368(c) IMMEDIATELY AFTER transfer
Property transferred (not services per §351(d)(1) — services don't qualify; receive stock taxable as comp)
Boot received → recognize gain up to FMV of boot per §351(b); character preserved (LTCG if held > 1 yr)
Transferor basis in stock = transferred property basis − boot + gain recognized (§358)
Corporation basis in property = transferor basis + gain recognized (§362)
Disqualified property: investment company stock (§351(e)(1) — Sec 351 'investment company' rules); inventory + receivables = ordinary
Distinct from §721 partnership contribution (similar rules but partnership; no 80% control test)
Coordinate with §1244 small biz stock election (G1.40) — ordinary loss treatment if business fails
QSBS §1202 timing — 5-yr holding starts at issuance (so §351 timing matters for QSBS clock)
State conformity varies — most follow federal §351 deferral
strategies.action: If forming new corp + contributing appreciated property: structure as §351 tax-free transfer (≥ 80% control). Defer gain on ~{{contributedFmv}} contribution. Estimated PV benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: §163(h)(3) acquisition vs equity indebtedness — mortgage interest deduction optimization
strategies.trigger: itemizedDeductions != null AND mortgage_interest adjustment > $20,000 (high mortgage interest = large loan).
strategies.formula: §163(h)(3)(F) TCJA changes (TY2018+): acquisition-indebtedness cap reduced from $1M to $750,000 (pre-TCJA $1M GRANDFATHERED). Home-equity indebtedness deduction REPEALED unless used to 'buy, build, or substantially improve' qualifying residence. Strategy: re-classify HELOC interest IF proceeds traced to acquisition/improvement. Heuristic: $30k mortgage interest × 80% deductible after cap × 24% marginal = $5,760 retained.
prerequisiteData: Mortgage origination date — loans before 2017-12-15 GRANDFATHERED at $1M acquisition cap per §163(h)(3)(F)(i)(I)
Loan use TRACING per Reg §1.163-8T — proceeds must be traced to USE (acquisition, improvement, equity)
Acquisition indebtedness per §163(h)(3)(B): debt incurred to acquire, construct, or substantially improve qualified residence; secured by qualified residence
Home-equity indebtedness post-TCJA: deductible ONLY if proceeds used to buy/build/improve (per TCJA §11043(a)(3))
Qualifying residence per §163(h)(4)(A)(i): principal residence + ONE other (vacation home OR rental used > 14 days personal)
Refinance grandfathering: refi of grandfathered loan stays at $1M cap UP TO original balance (§163(h)(3)(F)(iii))
Joint return aggregation — combined $750k cap (MFS $375k)
Coordinate with G1.3 bunching (itemized), G1.78 multi-state
OBBBA (2025-07-04) made $750k acquisition cap PERMANENT — TCJA sunset no longer applies
OBBBA TEMPORARILY raised SALT cap to $40,000 (2025-2029) for AGI < $500,000 — coordinate with G1.2 PTET + G1.85 mortgage stacking
strategies.action: Verify mortgage classification: acquisition (cap $750k post-2017) vs equity (deductible ONLY if buy/build/improve). Re-classify HELOC interest where traceable. Estimated retained deduction ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Charitable Lead Trust (CLT) — grantor CLT for income-tax deduction
strategies.irsPub: Pub 526; Rev. Proc. 2007-45 (CLT sample forms); Rev. Proc. 2008-45
strategies.trigger: adjustedGrossIncome > $1,000,000 (HNW) AND federalMarginalRate >= 0.32 AND charitableCash > $50,000 (genuine charitable intent).
strategies.formula: GRANTOR CLT — donor receives IMMEDIATE income-tax deduction for present value of charity's annuity lead interest (uses §7520 rate). Grantor pays tax on trust income during term (drawback). At term-end, remainder reverts to donor's heirs (NOT charity — distinct from CRT G1.19). Heuristic: $1M funded trust × 20-yr term × 5% annuity × 70% PV factor = $700,000 deduction × 37% marginal = $259,000 immediate tax savings (capped at 30% AGI for cash, 5-yr carryforward).
prerequisiteData: GRANTOR CLT vs Non-grantor CLT distinction: GRANTOR gives donor immediate income-tax deduction BUT donor pays tax on trust income; Non-grantor gives no immediate deduction but trust pays its own tax
Trust term + annuity rate determine PV deduction per §7520 rate (published monthly by IRS — use lowest available rate for highest deduction)
Qualifying charity: §170(c) public charity OR §509(a)(3) supporting org OR private foundation (different AGI caps)
AGI cap: 30% for cash, 20% for LTCG property to public charity (50%/30% for non-CLT) — DIFFERENT for CLT due to §170(f)(2)(B) coordination
5-yr carryforward per §170(d)(1) if exceeds AGI cap
Trust documents per Rev. Proc. 2007-45 sample forms (CLAT) or Rev. Proc. 2008-45 (CLUT)
Estate planning bonus — at trust end, remainder to heirs at REDUCED gift/estate tax value (frozen at funding date)
Distinct from CRT G1.19 (which is INCOME for donor, charity gets remainder) — CLT is OPPOSITE (charity gets income, donor's heirs get remainder)
OUT OF SCOPE for engine — engine doesn't compute §7520 rate × PV math; heuristic ROUGH estimate only
State conformity varies — most follow federal §170 limits
strategies.action: For HNW + charitable intent + multi-generational estate planning: fund $1M+ Grantor CLT. Immediate income-tax deduction ~{{deductionAmount}} × marginal = estimated ~{{estSavings}} tax savings (subject to 30% AGI cap).

strategies.name: §401(a)(17) compensation cap — high earners exceed qualified plan cap
strategies.irsPub: Pub 560 (Retirement Plans for Small Business); Notice 2023-75
strategies.trigger: totalIncome > $400,000 AND (W-2 wages OR netSeIncome) > §401(a)(17) cap ($345k TY2024 / $350k TY2025 / $360k TY2026) AND existing qualified retirement plan adjustment.
strategies.formula: §401(a)(17) annual compensation cap: $345,000 TY2024 (Notice 2023-75) / $350,000 TY2025 (Notice 2024-80) / $360,000 TY2026 (Notice 2025-67 / IR-2025-111). Compensation above cap is INELIGIBLE for qualified-plan contribution math (employer 401(k) match + profit-share + SEP). Strategy: combine qualified plan (cap-limited) with NQDC §409A (G1.57) for additional comp deferral. Cash balance plan G1.28 also extends defined-benefit limits. Heuristic (TY2024 illustration): $400k W-2 − $345k cap × 5% match = $2,750 lost match — recapture via NQDC or §415(b) defined benefit.
prerequisiteData: Employer matching formula (% × first X of comp — cap applies)
§415(c) defined-contribution annual additions limit: TY2024 $69,000 (employee deferral + employer match + profit-share); TY2025 $70,000
§415(b) defined-benefit annual benefit limit: TY2024 $275,000 (Notice 2023-75); TY2025 $280,000
If exceeded, consider: NQDC §409A (G1.57), defined-benefit/cash-balance plan (G1.28), Mega-Backdoor (G1.16)
Top-heavy testing per §416 — concentrated ownership may force minimum contribution
Highly Compensated Employees (HCE) test per §414(q): TY2024 > $155,000 (Notice 2023-75)
Coordinate with G1.16 mega-backdoor Roth + G1.57 NQDC §409A + G1.28 DB plan
strategies.action: High earner exceeds §401(a)(17) ${{capLimit}} cap. Add NQDC §409A (G1.57) or defined-benefit/cash-balance (G1.28) for additional deferral. Estimated annual benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: §199A SSTB navigation — Specified Service Trade or Business phase-out
strategies.irsPub: Form 8995-A; Notice 2023-75 (TY2024) / Notice 2024-80 (TY2025) / Rev. Proc. 2025-32 (TY2026) SSTB thresholds. OBBBA (P.L. 119-21) made §199A PERMANENT and widened the TY2026 phase-out window to $75k single / $150k MFJ.
strategies.trigger: K-1 active income > $50,000 OR netSeIncome > $50,000 AND adjustments include qbi_income AND taxableIncome between SSTB phase-in thresholds (year-indexed).
strategies.formula: §199A(d)(2) SSTB definition: health, law, accounting, actuarial sci, performing arts, consulting, athletics, financial services, brokerage, investment management, trading, dealing in securities/partnership interests/commodities, OR any trade where principal asset is reputation/skill of one or more employees. SSTB deduction phases OUT over the window ABOVE the threshold: $50k single / $100k MFJ through TY2025 (threshold $191,950/$383,900 TY2024; $197,300/$394,600 TY2025), and $75k single / $150k MFJ for TY2026 (threshold $201,750/$403,500; OBBBA widened the window) per §199A(d)(3) — fully phased out above the top. Strategy: stay BELOW threshold (defer income, accelerate deductions, max retirement) OR aggregate per Reg §1.199A-4 (G1.89). Heuristic: $50k SSTB QBI × 20% × 24% marginal = $2,400 preserved.
prerequisiteData: SSTB classification per Reg §1.199A-5 (8 enumerated categories + 'reputation/skill' catch-all)
TY2024 thresholds (Notice 2023-75): full deduction ≤ $191,950 single / $383,900 MFJ; phase-out $241,950 single / $483,900 MFJ; NO deduction above phase-out for SSTB
TY2025 thresholds (Notice 2024-80): full deduction ≤ $197,300 single / $394,600 MFJ; phase-out $247,300 single / $494,600 MFJ
TY2026 thresholds (Rev. Proc. 2025-32): full deduction ≤ $201,750 single / $403,500 MFJ; phase-out $276,750 single / $553,500 MFJ (OBBBA widened the window to $75k / $150k)
Non-SSTB businesses: phase-IN W-2-wage / UBIA cap above same threshold (G1.7 covers heuristic)
Strategy to stay below: max retirement (G1.1 SEP / G1.28 DB / G1.16 Mega), accelerate deductions (G1.3 bunching), defer income (G1.69)
Aggregation election per Reg §1.199A-4 (G1.89): combine related trades to share W-2 wages + UBIA
S-corp reasonable comp (G1.17) interaction: shifting more income to W-2 reduces SE but ALSO reduces QBI
Coordinate with G1.7 (W-2/UBIA simplified) + G1.89 (aggregation)
strategies.action: Client in SSTB phase-out range. Stay BELOW {{sstThreshold}} via max retirement + deduction acceleration. Preserved §199A benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: §199A aggregation election — combine related trades for W-2/UBIA cap
strategies.irsPub: Form 8995-A; Reg §1.199A-4(b) aggregation rules. §199A threshold above which the wage/UBIA cap binds is year-indexed ($191,950/$383,900 TY2024; $197,300/$394,600 TY2025; $201,750/$403,500 TY2026). OBBBA (P.L. 119-21) made §199A permanent.
strategies.trigger: K-1 active income > $100,000 AND adjustments include qbi_income AND taxableIncome above the year-indexed §199A phase-out threshold AND multiple businesses signaled (W-2 wage > $50k somewhere).
strategies.formula: Reg §1.199A-4(b) aggregation election: combine related non-SSTB trades/businesses to SHARE W-2 wages + UBIA caps. Same person/group must own ≥ 50%; trades must share at least 2 of (a) products/services, (b) facilities/employees, (c) operate in interconnected processes. Useful when one trade has wages/UBIA + another doesn't — aggregation lets the cap apply across both. Heuristic: $200k QBI × 20% × 24% = $9,600 preserved that would otherwise hit wage cap.
prerequisiteData: Common ownership ≥ 50% per Reg §1.199A-4(b)(1)(i) (attribution rules §267(b)/§707(b))
Shared characteristics ≥ 2 of: (a) products/services, (b) facilities/employees, (c) interconnected business processes
Cannot include SSTB in aggregation per Reg §1.199A-4(b)(1)(iv)
Election made on Form 8995-A Sch B; ANNUAL election (can re-aggregate each year)
Disclosure required per Reg §1.199A-4(c)(2) — disclose aggregation by trade
Aggregated wages + UBIA cap applies to AGGREGATED QBI (combined math, not per-trade)
Coordinate with G1.7 (W-2/UBIA simplified) + G1.88 (SSTB phase-out)
S-corp comp (G1.17) interaction
strategies.action: Aggregate ~{{numTrades}} related non-SSTB trades on Form 8995-A Sch B to share W-2/UBIA cap. Estimated preserved §199A benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Pooled Income Fund §642(c)(5) — charitable income trust
strategies.trigger: adjustedGrossIncome > $500,000 AND charitable_cash > $25,000 AND taxpayerAge >= 55 (income-need horizon).
strategies.formula: §642(c)(5) PIF — donor contributes property to charity-maintained fund; receives PRO-RATA income for life; remainder at death to charity. Similar to CRT G1.19 BUT (a) charity maintains fund (no separate trust admin cost); (b) lower minimum entry (~$5k vs $100k+ CRT); (c) pro-rata income (no fixed-rate annuity guarantee). PV deduction per §7520 rate × actuarial life table. Heuristic: $100k contribution × 70% PV remainder factor (age 70 + 7520 rate 5%) × 30% marginal = $21,000 immediate income-tax deduction (capped at 30% AGI for cash to public charity).
prerequisiteData: Qualified charity that maintains PIF per Reg §1.642(c)-5 (universities, hospitals, large land trusts commonly do)
Property contribution (cash, securities, real estate — appraisal req'd if > $5k non-cash per §170(f)(11)(C))
Donor receives life income (pro-rata share of fund's ordinary income) — taxable to donor as DNI per §662
PV remainder per §7520 rate × life-expectancy table — deductible to donor at contribution (§170(f)(2)(A))
AGI cap: 30% for cash to public charity (50%/30% for non-CRT); 5-yr carryforward per §170(d)(1)
Distinct from G1.19 CRT (separate trust, higher admin cost, fixed-rate annuity OR unitrust)
Income shares pro-rata to NEW contributions (rebalanced annually based on units)
OUT OF SCOPE for engine — PIF computes its own §662 income distribution; engine doesn't model. Heuristic ROUGH ESTIMATE only.
Coordinate with G1.11 QCD + G1.19 CRT + G1.86 CLT (all charitable income structures)
Estate planning bonus — contribution removes asset from gross estate per §2055
strategies.action: Contribute ${{contribution}} of appreciated property to a charity-maintained PIF. Receives pro-rata life income + immediate ~{{deductionAmount}} income-tax deduction. Estimated tax savings ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: §139 Qualified Disaster Relief — federal disaster declaration
strategies.irsPub: Pub 547 (Casualties, Disasters, Thefts); Pub 4492; FEMA disaster declaration database
strategies.trigger: Resident state in [CA, FL, TX, LA, NC, SC, TN, KY, MO, IA, GA] (recent federally-declared disaster states 2023-2025 proxy) AND adjustedGrossIncome > $100,000 AND no existing §139 adjustment.
strategies.formula: §139 — payments to an individual to reimburse/pay disaster-related expenses are EXCLUDED from gross income IF the disaster is FEDERALLY DECLARED per Stafford Act §401. Strategy: confirm any received payments (employer, charity, gov) are §139-eligible. Personal use of property destroyed: §165(h) casualty loss for federally-declared disaster (10% AGI floor waived for federally-declared disaster per §165(h)(2)(A)). Heuristic: $20,000 received disaster payment × 24% marginal = $4,800 saved by claiming §139 exclusion.
prerequisiteData: FEMA declaration database (fema.gov/disasters) confirms FEDERALLY-DECLARED disaster (per Stafford Act §401)
Excludable per §139(b): reasonable/necessary personal/family/living/funeral expenses incurred as result of disaster
Excludable per §139(c)(2): reasonable/necessary expenses for repair/rehabilitation of personal residence/contents
Excludable per §139(c)(3): by reason of death/personal physical injury
MUST be paid AS RESULT of qualified disaster (timing + causation)
§165(h)(5)(A) federally-declared disaster loss: deductible above 10% AGI floor (waived for fed-declared per TCJA §11044 — extends through 2025)
Casualty loss claim: Form 4684; basis adjustment per §165(b)
Coordinate with disaster-specific elections (e.g., §165(i) prior-year deduction election for federally-declared)
Specific exclusions: §139(c)(4)(A) - not eligible for §139 if payment also Code §164/§115 excludable
State conformity varies
strategies.action: Check FEMA database for federally-declared disaster in your state. Confirm any received payments excluded under §139. Document any disaster loss for §165(h) casualty deduction. Estimated benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Solo 401(k) employee deferral — beats SEP for low-to-mid SE
strategies.trigger: netSeIncome BETWEEN $20,000 AND $150,000 AND no existing Solo 401(k) adjustment AND no existing SEP-IRA adjustment AND filingStatus != married_filing_separately.
strategies.formula: Solo 401(k) = employee deferral + employer match. Employee deferral $23,000 TY2024 / $23,500 TY2025 / $24,500 TY2026 (§402(g)) + age 50+ catch-up $7,500 ($8,000 TY2026) (§414(v)). Employer match = 20% × (netSE − halfSE) (same as SEP); total capped at the §415(c) limit ($69k/$70k/$72k). For low-mid SE (~$50k netSE), employee deferral ALONE exceeds SEP's full 20% calc. Heuristic: $50k netSE × ratio comparison: SEP = $9,300 vs Solo 401(k) = $23,000 + match = ~$30k → $20k MORE shelter × marginal = $4,400.
prerequisiteData: Plan administration cost (Solo 401(k) ~$1k/yr vs SEP ~$0); Form 5500-EZ at $250k+ assets
Employer match calculation same as SEP: 20% × (netSE − halfSE) per §415(c) annual cap
Total §415(c) annual additions cap TY2024 $69,000 / TY2025 $70,000
Plan establishment deadline: 12-31 of plan year (vs SEP-IRA extended return deadline = much more flexible)
Solo 401(k) allows Roth designation (Solo Roth 401(k) - tax-free growth), SEP doesn't
Loan provision per §72(p) - Solo 401(k) can offer; SEP-IRA cannot
Coordinate with G1.1 (SEP-IRA) — choose based on SE income level + plan-admin tolerance
strategies.action: Open Solo 401(k) instead of SEP-IRA before Dec 31. Employee deferral ${{employeeDeferral}} + employer match ${{employerMatch}} = ${{totalContribution}} shelter. Extra vs SEP ~${{extraVsSep}}; estimated savings ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: §163(d)(4)(B) election — treat QDIV/LTCG as ordinary for investment interest deduction
strategies.trigger: qualifiedDividends + longTermGains > $20,000 AND adjustments include investment_interest_expense > $5,000.
strategies.formula: §163(d) caps investment-interest expense at NET INVESTMENT INCOME. Default: net investment income EXCLUDES QDIV + net LTCG (taxed at preferential rates). §163(d)(4)(B) ELECTION: include QDIV + net LTCG in net investment income (giving up preferential rate on that portion in exchange for full investment-interest deduction). Beneficial when investment-interest expense > ordinary investment income AND lost-LTCG-preferential < retained-interest-deduction. Heuristic: $20k QDIV/LTCG × (37% ord − 23.8% LTCG+NIIT) = $2,640 'paid' to free up matching $20k interest deduction × 37% = $7,400 'gained' → net $4,760.
prerequisiteData: Form 4952 to track + report investment interest expense
Investment interest expense per §163(d)(3) — interest on debt to PURCHASE / CARRY investment property
Net investment income per §163(d)(4): ordinary investment income (interest, royalties, net ST gain) − investment expenses
Excess investment interest carries forward indefinitely per §163(d)(2)
Election made annually on Form 4952 Line 4g — irrevocable for that year
Beneficial when marginal rate >> LTCG rate AND investment-interest expense is large
Coordinate with G1.6 NIIT (electing in adds to NIIT base too)
Trade-or-business interest is §163(j) territory (G1 catalog C7) — different rules
strategies.action: If investment-interest expense ${{investmentInt}} > net investment income: elect §163(d)(4)(B) on Form 4952 Line 4g to include ${{electedAmount}} of QDIV/LTCG as ordinary (frees up matching interest deduction). Estimated net benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: §85 unemployment income — confirm no remaining ARP exclusion
strategies.trigger: 1099-G unemployment income > $5,000 AND adjustedGrossIncome < $150,000.
strategies.formula: §85: unemployment income FULLY federal-taxable post-TY2020. American Rescue Plan §9042 (TY2020-only) excluded first $10,200 of unemployment for AGI < $150k — SUNSET. State conformity varies (some states still exclude). Strategy: (a) confirm engine correctly includes 100%; (b) elect §3402(p) voluntary 10% withholding on UI to avoid §6654 underpayment penalty; (c) state-specific exclusions (TX, WA, FL have no income tax; CA excludes 100%; NJ excludes 100%; PA excludes 100%; some states partial). Heuristic: $15k UI × 12% marginal = $1,800 — plus avoid underpayment penalty.
prerequisiteData: 1099-G Box 1 unemployment compensation amount
§85 unemployment income FULLY federal-taxable per TY2021+ (post-ARP sunset)
Voluntary withholding election per §3402(p) — 10% flat rate from state UI office (Form W-4V)
State conformity: TX/WA/FL/NV/SD/WY/AK no income tax; CA/NJ/PA/MT/VA/AL/NH exclude UI; OR excludes after age 62; partial exclusion AR/AL/HI/IN/IA/KY/LA/ME/MD/MI/MS/MO/NE/NM/NY/ND/OK/RI/SC/UT/VT/WV/WI
Schedule 1 Line 7 — full UI amount
Form W-4V — voluntary withholding (10% flat federal rate ONLY — no state withholding option for UI)
§6654 underpayment penalty — UI without withholding can trigger penalty even with low income
Coordinate with G1.52 estimated tax safe harbor + state-specific filing
strategies.action: Verify ${{uiAmount}} unemployment income correctly reported on Sch 1 Line 7. Elect §3402(p) voluntary 10% withholding via Form W-4V to avoid §6654 penalty. State-specific exclusion review. Estimated benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: §1377(a)(2) S-corp terminating shareholder year-end close election
strategies.trigger: K-1 from s_corp with active K-1 income > $50,000 AND netSeIncome > $50,000 (proxy for client being engaged in S-corp).
strategies.formula: §1377(a)(2) election: when terminating shareholder's interest mid-year, S-corp can elect to CLOSE BOOKS on date of termination (split tax year into 2 short periods). Default per §1377(a)(1) is pro-rata allocation by days. Beneficial when income/expense timing differs between halves (e.g., terminating shareholder leaves in low-revenue period; default allocates pro-rata so they get extra income). Heuristic: $200k typical mismatch × 24% marginal = $48,000 character (case-specific).
prerequisiteData: Termination event per §1377(a)(2): shareholder disposes ENTIRE interest in S-corp (sale, redemption, gift, death)
Election BY ALL AFFECTED shareholders (terminating + continuing) per Reg §1.1377-1(b)(2)
Reg §1.1377-1(b)(3) election made on Form 1120-S; statement attached identifying termination date
Books closed as of termination date — produces 2 short tax years for K-1 allocation
Default = pro-rata per §1377(a)(1) — allocate by days of ownership
Coordinate with §1362 S-corp election (termination of S election is different); §1377 is mid-year SHAREHOLDER termination
§1366(a) flow-through unchanged either way; only ALLOCATION method differs
Coordinate with G1.83 §338(h)(10) (full sale election) + G1.17 reasonable comp
All affected shareholders must consent — unanimous OR fall back to pro-rata
strategies.action: If S-corp shareholder terminates mid-year: file §1377(a)(2) election (Form 1120-S statement) with ALL affected shareholders' consent. Estimated character benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: §132(f) Qualified Transportation Fringe — employer commuter benefits
strategies.irsPub: Pub 15-B (Employer's Tax Guide to Fringe Benefits); Notice 2023-75
strategies.trigger: netSeIncome > $100,000 (proxy for biz that may sponsor) OR W-2 wages exist (employee eligible).
strategies.formula: §132(f) qualified transportation fringe — EMPLOYEE excludes up to monthly cap × 12 from gross income for: (a) transit pass + commuter highway vehicle pass per §132(f)(2)(A) TY2024 $315/mo (Notice 2023-75); (b) qualified parking per §132(f)(2)(B) TY2024 $315/mo; (c) qualified bicycle commuting per §132(f)(2)(C) SUSPENDED TY2018-TY2025 by TCJA §11047. Heuristic: $315/mo × 12 × 24% marginal = $907 employee tax savings; employer cost ${{315 × 12 = $3,780}} (BUT post-TCJA §132(f) NOT deductible by employer for transit/parking — pure employee benefit).
prerequisiteData: TY2024 monthly cap $315 (transit + parking each, Notice 2023-75)
TY2025 monthly cap $325 (Notice 2024-80)
Transit pass per §132(f)(5)(A) — token, fare card, voucher, similar instrument
Commuter highway vehicle per §132(f)(5)(B) — vehicle seat ≥ 6 adults; 50%+ of mileage transports employees between home + workplace
Qualified parking per §132(f)(5)(C) — on/near employer premises or near transit terminal employee uses for commute
Bicycle commuting reimbursement per §132(f)(5)(F) — SUSPENDED TY2018-TY2025 by TCJA §11047
TCJA §13304 repealed EMPLOYER deduction for §132(f) (other than safety) — pure employee benefit now
Compensation-reduction election per §132(f)(4) — employee pre-tax election (no compensation cost to employer)
Coordinate with §125 cafeteria plan (different mechanism — typically health + dependent care)
State conformity varies (NY/CA may differ on parking)
strategies.action: If employer offers §132(f) qualified transit/parking benefits, elect ${{monthlyAmount}}/mo pre-tax. TY${{taxYear}} cap ${{capPerMo}}/mo each. Estimated annual employee tax savings ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: No tax on tips — qualified tips deduction (OBBBA §224)
strategies.irsPub: IRS 'OBBB tax deductions for working Americans and seniors'; Schedule 1-A (Form 1040). NEW above-the-line deduction, TY2025–2028 only.
strategies.trigger: qualified_tips adjustment > 0 AND taxYear 2025–2028 AND MAGI below the phase-out ceiling (single ~$400k / MFJ ~$550k).
strategies.formula: deduction = min(qualifiedTips, $25,000), reduced by $100 per $1,000 of MAGI over $150,000 single / $300,000 MFJ (floored at 0). estSavings = deduction × (federal + state marginal rate). Available to itemizers AND non-itemizers.
prerequisiteData: Qualified-tip amount (W-2 box 7 / 8 + employer reporting; new info-reporting under §6041 etc.)
Occupation customarily + regularly received tips on/before 2024-12-31 (Treasury TTOC list); SSTB workers excluded
MAGI (drives the $100-per-$1,000 phase-out over $150k single / $300k MFJ)
strategies.action: Deduct qualified tips above-the-line under OBBBA §224 — deductible portion ~{{deduction}} this year (cap $25,000, phasing out over MAGI). Estimated tax savings ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: No tax on overtime — qualified overtime deduction (OBBBA §225)
strategies.irsPub: IRS Q&A on qualified overtime compensation; Notice 2025-69; Schedule 1-A (Form 1040). NEW above-the-line deduction, TY2025–2028 only.
strategies.trigger: qualified_overtime adjustment > 0 AND taxYear 2025–2028 AND MAGI below the phase-out ceiling.
strategies.formula: deduction = min(qualifiedOvertime, $12,500 single / $25,000 MFJ), reduced by $100 per $1,000 of MAGI over $150,000 single / $300,000 MFJ (floored at 0). Only the FLSA premium 'half' (pay in excess of the regular rate) qualifies — not the full time-and-a-half. estSavings = deduction × (federal + state marginal rate).
prerequisiteData: Qualified-overtime amount = the FLSA §7 premium portion only (pay above the regular rate); per W-2 box reporting under the new OBBBA rules
MAGI (drives the $100-per-$1,000 phase-out over $150k single / $300k MFJ)
strategies.action: Deduct the FLSA overtime premium above-the-line under OBBBA §225 — deductible portion ~{{deduction}} this year (cap $12,500 single / $25,000 MFJ). Estimated tax savings ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Car loan interest deduction — new US-assembled vehicle (OBBBA §163(h)(4))
strategies.irsPub: IRS 'OBBB tax deductions for working Americans and seniors'; Federal Register car-loan-interest regs; Schedule 1-A (Form 1040). NEW above-the-line deduction, TY2025–2028 only.
strategies.trigger: qualified_car_loan_interest adjustment > 0 AND taxYear 2025–2028 AND MAGI below the phase-out ceiling (single $150k / MFJ $250k).
strategies.formula: deduction = min(carLoanInterest, $10,000), reduced by $200 per $1,000 of MAGI over $100,000 single / $200,000 MFJ (DOUBLE the tips/overtime rate; floored at 0). estSavings = deduction × (federal + state marginal rate). Available to itemizers AND non-itemizers.
prerequisiteData: Interest paid on a qualifying auto loan (loan originated after 2024-12-31, secured by a first lien on the vehicle, VIN reported)
Vehicle is NEW (original use begins with taxpayer), personal-use, FINAL ASSEMBLY IN THE U.S. — leases + used vehicles do NOT qualify
MAGI (drives the $200-per-$1,000 phase-out over $100k single / $200k MFJ)
strategies.action: Deduct qualified passenger-vehicle loan interest above-the-line under OBBBA §163(h)(4) — deductible portion ~{{deduction}} this year (cap $10,000). Estimated tax savings ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Senior bonus deduction — age 65+ ($6,000) (OBBBA)
strategies.irsPub: IRS 'OBBB tax deductions for working Americans and seniors'. NEW above-the-line deduction, TY2025–2028 only; stacks on the existing age-65 additional standard deduction.
strategies.trigger: taxpayerAge >= 65 (or spouseAge >= 65 for MFJ) AND taxYear 2025–2028 AND MAGI below the full phase-out ($175k single / $350k MFJ for two seniors).
strategies.formula: deduction = $6,000 × (number of individuals age 65+), reduced by 6% of MAGI over $75,000 single / $150,000 MFJ (floored at 0). estSavings = deduction × (federal + state marginal rate). Available to itemizers AND non-itemizers; on TOP of the existing age-65 additional standard deduction.
prerequisiteData: Taxpayer (and/or spouse) is age 65+ by year-end
MAGI (drives the 6%-of-excess phase-out over $75k single / $150k MFJ)
strategies.action: Claim the OBBBA senior bonus deduction ($6,000 per individual age 65+) — deductible portion ~{{deduction}} this year after the MAGI phase-out. Estimated tax savings ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Annual-exclusion gifting (estate reduction)
strategies.irsPub: Rev. Proc. 2023-34 ($18k 2024); Rev. Proc. 2024-40 ($19k 2025); Rev. Proc. 2025-32 ($19k 2026). Basic exclusion amount (BEA) $13.61M 2024 / $13.99M 2025 / $15M 2026 (OBBBA-permanent).
strategies.trigger: Affluent-estate proxy: AGI >= $400k OR net investment income >= $100k.
strategies.formula: estSavings = annual gift exclusion x 40% top estate rate (one donee/year removed from a taxable estate, plus its future appreciation). Per donee, per year; MFJ gift-splitting doubles it. Only benefits an estate above the federal BEA or a lower state estate-tax threshold.
prerequisiteData: Total estate / net worth vs the federal BEA (and any state estate/inheritance threshold)
Number of intended donees (children, grandchildren, others)
MFJ gift-splitting consent (doubles the per-donee exclusion; requires Form 709)
strategies.action: Gift up to {{annualExclusion}} per donee this year (gift-tax-free under §2503(b)) — removes that amount plus its future appreciation from a taxable estate. Per-donee estate-tax reduction ~{{estSavings}} at the 40% rate; MFJ gift-splitting doubles the exclusion.

strategies.name: 529 plan superfunding (5-year gift election)
strategies.irsPub: Form 709; Rev. Proc. 2024-40. Front-loads 5x the annual exclusion; SECURE 2.0 added a limited 529->Roth rollover for long-held accounts.
strategies.trigger: Has dependents AND (AGI >= $300k OR net investment income >= $50k).
strategies.formula: Front-load 5 x annual exclusion ($90k 2024 / $95k 2025-26) into a 529 with the §529(c)(2)(B) election. estSavings = illustrative income-tax-free growth value = superfund x ((1.06^10 - 1)) x 15% LTCG (10-yr, 6% growth assumption).
prerequisiteData: Beneficiary (child/grandchild) and current 529 balance
State 529 income-tax deduction/credit (varies by state)
Whether the donor expects to survive the 5-year election period (else partial estate add-back)
strategies.action: Front-load {{superfund}} into a 529 plan using the §529(c)(2)(B) 5-year election — gift-tax-free and out of the estate immediately. Illustrative income-tax-free-growth value ~{{estSavings}} (6%/10-yr assumption).

strategies.name: Spousal Lifetime Access Trust (SLAT)
strategies.irsPub: Form 709. Uses the donor-spouse's BEA ($13.99M 2025 / $15M 2026 OBBBA-permanent); the beneficiary spouse retains indirect access.
strategies.trigger: MFJ AND (AGI >= $750k OR net investment income >= $250k).
strategies.formula: Illustrative annual estate-tax shelter on an assumed $1M of trust assets appreciating 6%/yr, removed from a 40% estate: $1,000,000 x 6% x 40% = $24,000/yr. CPA sizes to actual funding.
prerequisiteData: Assets available to fund the trust (and not needed for the grantor's own support)
Whether both spouses fund SLATs (reciprocal-trust doctrine risk if identical)
Estate value vs the BEA ($30M for a couple)
strategies.action: Fund a Spousal Lifetime Access Trust to remove appreciating assets (and their growth) from the estate while the beneficiary spouse retains indirect access. Illustrative shelter ~{{estSavings}}/yr on a $1M illustrative funding — size to the client's balance sheet.

strategies.name: Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust (ILIT)
strategies.irsPub: Form 709 (Crummey-notice gifts). Removes the death benefit from the taxable estate when the ILIT (not the insured) owns the policy.
strategies.trigger: AGI >= $750k OR net investment income >= $250k.
strategies.formula: Illustrative: assumed $2M death benefit removed from a 40% estate, annualized over 25 yrs = $2,000,000 x 40% / 25 = $32,000/yr-equivalent. CPA sizes to the actual policy.
prerequisiteData: Existing/planned life-insurance face value and ownership
Whether transferring an EXISTING policy (triggers the §2035 3-year lookback)
Trustee + annual Crummey-notice administration capacity
strategies.action: Hold life insurance in an Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust so the death benefit escapes the taxable estate (§2042). Illustrative annualized estate-tax benefit ~{{estSavings}} on an assumed $2M face — size to the actual policy.

strategies.name: Grantor Retained Annuity Trust (GRAT)
strategies.irsPub: Form 709; §7520 rate (updated monthly). Transfers appreciation above the §7520 hurdle gift-tax-free.
strategies.trigger: (AGI >= $750k OR net investment income >= $250k) AND long-term capital gains >= $100k.
strategies.formula: Illustrative: assumed $1M funding; appreciation above the ~5.4% §7520 hurdle is removed. At 8% growth, excess = 2.6% x $1M = $26k/yr, x 40% estate = $10,400/yr.
prerequisiteData: Concentrated/appreciating asset suitable for a near-zeroed-out GRAT
Current §7520 rate (sets the hurdle the assets must beat)
Whether the grantor is likely to survive the GRAT term (mortality risk)
strategies.action: Fund a (near-zeroed-out) Grantor Retained Annuity Trust with appreciating assets — appreciation above the §7520 hurdle passes to heirs gift-tax-free. Illustrative benefit ~{{estSavings}}/yr (8% growth vs 5.4% hurdle on $1M) — size to the actual asset.

strategies.name: Step-up-in-basis hold (§1014)
strategies.irsPub: Pub 551. Inherited capital assets take a date-of-death FMV basis — the built-in gain is never income-taxed. IRD assets (IRAs, annuities) do NOT step up.
strategies.trigger: taxpayerAge >= 65 AND long-term capital gains >= $50k.
strategies.formula: estSavings = realized LTCG x 15% (illustrative — a comparable block of appreciated assets held until death avoids ~15% LTCG via the §1014 step-up). Community-property states get a double step-up.
prerequisiteData: Embedded (unrealized) gain in the client's taxable, non-retirement holdings
Whether the client needs liquidity/diversification that argues for selling anyway
Community-property status (double step-up for a surviving spouse)
strategies.action: For appreciated assets the client doesn't need to sell, consider HOLDING until death — heirs get a §1014 basis step-up to FMV and the built-in gain is never income-taxed. On the {{ltcg}} of gains realized this year, the step-up equivalent is ~{{estSavings}} of avoided LTCG.

strategies.name: Persistent NIIT exposure — structural advice
strategies.trigger: niitTax > 0 in current year AND niitTax > 0 in >= 1 prior year
strategies.formula: estSavings = avg(niitTax across years) * 0.5 (structural changes — entity restructure, loss harvesting, deferred-comp shifting — typically recover ~50% of recurring NIIT)
prerequisiteData: Investment income breakdown by source (dividends, interest, capital gains, rental)
Whether client has flexibility on entity structure (S-corp election, real-estate professional status)
Whether client can systematically harvest losses against capital gains each year
strategies.action: NIIT has applied for {{years}} consecutive years averaging ~{{avgNiit}}/year. Consider structural changes — entity restructure, systematic loss harvesting, deferred-comp timing, or moving income-producing assets into qualified retirement accounts. Estimated annual recoverable benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Persistent AMT exposure — preference review
strategies.trigger: amtTax > 0 in current year AND amtTax > 0 in >= 1 prior year
strategies.formula: estSavings = avg(amtTax across years) * 0.4 (AMT preferences — ISO timing, depreciation method, state-tax bunching — typically recover ~40% of recurring AMT)
prerequisiteData: Source of AMT preferences (ISO bargain, depreciation, state tax addback)
Whether client has flexibility on ISO exercise timing in future years
Whether client itemizes (state-tax bunching could shift AMT preference)
strategies.action: AMT has applied for {{years}} consecutive years averaging ~{{avgAmt}}/year. Schedule a Form 6251 review — most recurring AMT comes from ISO timing, depreciation method choice, or state-tax addback. Restructuring preferences across years can recover ~{{estSavings}}/year.

strategies.name: Permanent bunching strategy — persistent std-ded cliff
strategies.trigger: itemizedTotal within +/- 15% of stdDed in current AND >= 1 prior year (persistent cliff) AND charitableCash > 0 in current year
strategies.formula: estSavings = stdDed * 0.25 * marginalRate (single cycle benefit; pattern persistence raises confidence vs G1.3)
prerequisiteData: Whether client has flexibility to prepay 2 years of charitable cash in December
Whether client has flexibility on property-tax timing (acceleration)
Whether client wants to open a DAF account (vs direct charity prepayments)
strategies.action: Itemized deductions have hovered within +/- 15% of the standard-deduction threshold for {{years}} consecutive years. This is a permanent pattern — commit to a 2-year alternating bunching strategy (or open a DAF) to capture ~{{estSavings}}/year on average.

strategies.name: Capital loss carryforward unused — plan gain realization
strategies.trigger: (capitalLossCarryforwardShort + capitalLossCarryforwardLong) > $20,000 in current year AND has not declined materially YoY (delta <= $1k OR grew)
strategies.formula: estSavings = min(currentCarryforward, $20,000) * marginalRate (potential to offset up to $20k of realized capital gains using the carryforward)
prerequisiteData: Whether client has appreciated positions that could be realized
Whether client's investment strategy is consistent with realizing gains (rebalancing, gifting basis-stepped, sale)
Whether the carryforward is short-term or long-term (preferential rate matters)
strategies.action: Capital loss carryforward of ~{{carryforward}} has remained unused for {{years}} years. Plan to realize gains (rebalancing or specific-lot sales) to absorb up to $20k of the carryforward — estimated annual benefit ~{{estSavings}}.

strategies.name: Passive activity loss suspension growing — exit or activate
strategies.trigger: (scheduleEPassiveLossSuspended + k1PassiveLossSuspended) > $5,000 in current year AND has GROWN year-over-year (more suspended this year than last)
strategies.formula: estSavings = (currentSuspended - priorSuspended) * marginalRate * 0.5 (potential to release the year-over-year delta through disposition or qualifying as real-estate-professional)
prerequisiteData: Whether client has any passive INCOME activities to apply against the loss
Whether client would qualify for real-estate-professional status (750 hours, > 50% of personal services)
Whether client is willing to dispose of one passive activity to release that activity's suspended PAL
strategies.action: Passive losses suspended under §469 have grown by ~{{growth}} year-over-year (to ~{{currentSuspended}}). The suspended balance compounds annually without an exit. Consider: (a) full disposition of one activity to fully deduct THAT activity's suspended PAL, (b) qualifying as real-estate professional, or (c) adding a passive-income activity. Estimated annual benefit ~{{estSavings}}.
